notes: LinkedIn is JS-heavy; content was retrieved via a summarization fetch rather than raw HTML, so treat exact figures as approximate. A separate pass at the same URL also surfaced the sentence 'currently employ 14 team members,' i.e. a point estimate within the 11-50 band. Tracxn's company profile (tracxn.com/d/companies/toaststudios, fetched directly but too aggressively bot-blocked -- HTTP 429 -- to remain a stable citation for this ledger) separately put the India entity at 16 employees as of April 30, 2026, roughly consistent with this LinkedIn range; that same Tracxn page also stated Toast Studios 'has not raised any funding yet,' which conflicts with the PR Newswire-reported $1.6M pre-seed (wsa-funding-round) and Lightspeed's own portfolio listing (wsa-lightspeed-investment) -- most likely because Tracxn's funding tracker covers only the Indian operating subsidiary, not the U.S. Delaware entity that actually took the Lightspeed check. TIER CORRECTED B -> C: a company's own LinkedIn page (industry tag, size band, HQ) is self-published corporate profile data controlled by the company, not independent press or a VC's own disclosure about a portfolio company -- it belongs in the same category as playtoast.com marketing pages (tier C), not the 'independent press' or 'VC self-published' tier-B buckets. Content re-confirmed live via fetch 2026-07-14 (Industry: Mobile Gaming Apps; Company Size: 11-50 employees; Headquarters: New York), consistent with the original capture.

claim: In the three largest live legal iGaming states (Pennsylvania, Michigan, New Jersey), 2025 iGaming revenue averaged approximately $288 per resident per year.
sourceName: Workstream calculation (WS-B)
methodology: (PA 2025 iGaming revenue $3,463,152,378 [wsb-pa-igaming-2025] + MI $3,087,027,384 [wsb-mi-igaming-2025] + NJ $2,911,241,318 [wsb-nj-igaming-2025]) / (PA population 13,073,016 + MI population 10,155,806 + NJ population 9,590,076, all from wsb-population-igaming-states-2025) = $9,461,421,080 / 32,818,898 people ≈ $288.25 per capita. (A national-basis alternative using the full $10.74B iGaming total [wsb-aga-2025-igaming-revenue] over the 7-state active population of ~40.47M yields a lower ~$265/capita; the $288 per-capita figure from the 3 dominant states is used as the more representative 'mature market' benchmark since PA/MI/NJ represent ~90% of national volume and have operated the longest.)
notes: This per-capita benchmark is the key multiplier used in the TAM calculation below (wsb-tam-p2p-skill-gaming). It represents the revenue a mature, fully-legal, full-catalog (slots + live dealer + table games) iCasino product extracts per resident where it's legal -- a deliberately generous ceiling for a narrower P2P skill-gaming product. Cross-check note added during verification (2026-07-14): the PA/MI/NJ inputs to this calculation are 'gross revenue' basis figures (see corrected notes on wsb-pa-igaming-2025 and wsb-mi-igaming-2025); Pennsylvania's own regulator reports a lower net/taxable 'iGaming Revenue' ($2,775,554,529) that would reduce this per-capita estimate by roughly 6% if substituted, but the gross basis used here is consistent with AGA's national methodology and is retained.

claim: SAM (serviceable available market, tier D): using the AGA's own estimate of illegal online slots/table-games revenue ($18.6B/year) as a grounded proxy for proven, already-monetized-elsewhere demand for casino-style real-money gaming among people the legal iCasino industry does not serve, the realistic serviceable market for a legal P2P casino-style skill-gaming product is on the order of $15-19 billion/year.
sourceName: Workstream calculation (WS-B)
methodology: SAM = AGA's directly-measured illegal online slots + table games revenue estimate, $18.6B/year (wsb-aga-illegal-igaming), taken as-is rather than derived from the TAM figure above. Rationale: the AGA estimate already represents real, currently-collected revenue for casino-style RNG/table-game play happening outside the legal, licensed 7-8-state iCasino system -- i.e., proven willingness-to-pay for exactly the product category (casino-style games, played for real money, outside licensed iCasino) that a legal P2P skill-gaming alternative would compete for. It is a bottom-up, evidence-based number rather than a top-down per-capita extrapolation, which is why it is used directly as SAM instead of as an input to further multiplication. It is smaller than the $86B TAM ceiling (wsb-tam-p2p-skill-gaming) by roughly 4.6x, reflecting that only a fraction of the theoretical ceiling is actually being monetized by ANY channel (legal or illegal) today.
notes: This SAM also assumes the illegal-market number captures largely the same casino-style demand a P2P skill-gaming product targets; some of that $18.6B is illegal offshore *iCasino-style* sites (i.e., unlicensed copies of the same slots/table games regulated operators offer, not necessarily P2P/skill-framed products), so this should be read as an upper-bound-for-SAM proxy, not an exact fit.

claim: SOM (serviceable obtainable market, tier D): benchmarked strictly against the disclosed revenue range of the only SEC-reporting comparable in this category -- Skillz, whose annual revenue has ranged from a $95.5 million trough (2024) to a $384.1 million peak (2021) -- a realistic 3-5 year obtainable revenue range for a single leading P2P casino-style skill-gaming operator is approximately $50-400 million/year, or roughly 0.3%-2% of the $18.6B SAM.
sourceName: Workstream calculation (WS-B)
methodology: SOM range derived ONLY from tier-A anchors -- the disclosed annual-revenue history of Skillz, the sole SEC-reporting operator in this exact category: peak $384.1M in FY2021 (wsb-skillz-revenue-2021, tier A) and trough $95.5M in FY2024 (wsb-skillz-revenue-2024, tier A). Lower bound: 0.5 x Skillz's FY2024 trough (0.5 x $95.5M = $47.75M, rounded to $50M) -- a conservative case in which a new entrant reaches only about half the scale the incumbent retained even after a 75% collapse. Upper bound: ~1.04 x Skillz's all-time peak (1.04 x $384.1M = $399.5M, rounded to $400M) -- a case in which the new entrant slightly exceeds the best year the incumbent ever achieved, reflecting (but not quantifying) the diligence thesis that casino-style games with higher stakes monetize better per user than Solitaire-style tournaments. As a share of SAM: $50M / $18.6B = 0.27% and $400M / $18.6B = 2.15% (wsb-sam-p2p-skill-gaming), i.e., roughly 0.3%-2%. SENSITIVITY (not an anchor): Papaya Gaming's press-reported ~$150M/year (wsb-papaya-revenue-estimate, tier C, unconfirmed, 2021-dated) falls inside this range near its midpoint, which is weakly consistent with the range but is deliberately NOT used as an input because it is a tier-C figure and the brief requires tier-A/B inputs only. Neither comp operates the specific casino-style-plus-offshore-matching model described in diligence; both are Solitaire/casual-tournament players. This is explicitly a comp-based sanity check, not a bottoms-up operating forecast, and does not account for the material regulatory risk flagged elsewhere (wsb-aviagames-doj-subpoena, ws0-prosecutor-regulatory-risk) that could cap realized revenue well below this range or end the model entirely.
notes: The single biggest sensitivity in this SOM: Skillz -- the only SEC-reporting comp in this space -- has never exceeded $384M/year despite nearly 6 years as a public company, suggesting the top of this SOM range ($400M) may be optimistic without a materially different monetization mechanic (which the diligence claims Toast has, via the offshore-matching 'latency fix' enabling higher-stakes casino-style games). SOURCES.md's research lead of a Skillz 'peak market cap >$10B' could not be independently verified via any fetchable, quotable source in this session (stockanalysis.com no longer serves Skillz data post-rebrand to Firy Inc.; macrotrends.net returned HTTP 402 paywall) and is therefore NOT included as a fact or relied upon here -- flagged as an open item rather than cited unverified.

claim: TAM (theoretical ceiling, tier D): if P2P casino-style skill gaming in the ~42 non-iCasino states captured the SAME per-capita revenue that mature, full-catalog legal iGaming captures in PA/MI/NJ, the ceiling would be approximately $86 billion/year -- explicitly flagged as an unrealistic upper bound, not a real near-term forecast.
sourceName: Workstream calculation (WS-B)
methodology: Non-iCasino-state population (~299.9M, wsb-nonicasino-population-estimate [tier D], itself derived from wsb-us-population-2025 [tier A, US Census] minus wsb-population-igaming-states-2025 [tier B, Census-compiled state figures]) x mature-market iGaming revenue-per-capita benchmark ($288.25, wsb-igaming-revenue-per-capita [tier D], itself derived from wsb-pa-igaming-2025 + wsb-mi-igaming-2025 + wsb-nj-igaming-2025 [all tier B, state-reported 2025 revenue] over the same three states' populations from wsb-population-igaming-states-2025 [tier B]) = $86.5 billion/year. Full underlying A/B lineage: wsb-us-population-2025 (A), wsb-population-igaming-states-2025 (B), wsb-pa-igaming-2025 (B), wsb-mi-igaming-2025 (B), wsb-nj-igaming-2025 (B). TREAT AS A CEILING, NOT A FORECAST: this assumes (a) 100% substitution -- every non-iCasino-state resident spends on P2P skill gaming exactly as much as a PA/MI/NJ resident spends on the full legal iCasino catalog (slots, live dealer, table games, poker), which a narrower peer-to-peer/skill-only product (blackjack- and craps-style games only, per diligence) could not realistically replicate; and (b) no regulatory action shuts the model down before it scales (a live, material risk per wsb-aviagames-doj-subpoena and ws0-prosecutor-regulatory-risk). This TAM figure exists to establish the outer bound of the addressable opportunity, not as a revenue projection.
notes: Cross-check: this ~$86B TAM ceiling is comparable in order of magnitude to the (dated, low-confidence) 2021 Goldman Sachs all-50-states iGaming+sports-betting forecast of $40B (wsb-ekg-goldman-2021-forecast) and to DraftKings' own reported TAM-expansion narrative context (wsb-draftkings-prediction-market-tam), suggesting the ~$40-90B range is a recurring order-of-magnitude estimate across multiple, methodologically distinct approaches for 'what US gambling looks like if every state effectively had it.'

claim: Apple App Store Review Guideline 5.3.4 requires that real-money gaming apps (sports betting, poker, casino games, horse racing) hold necessary licenses/permissions in every location where used, be geo-restricted to only those locations, be free to download, and prohibits use of in-app purchase to buy gaming credit/currency (third-party processors like PayPal/Apple Pay are permitted instead).
sourceName: Apple Developer Forums (quoting App Store Review Guidelines §5.3.3-5.3.4)
quote: 5.3.3 Apps may not use in-app purchase to purchase credit or currency for use in conjunction with real money gaming of any kind, and may not enable people to purchase lottery or raffle tickets or initiate fund transfers in the app. ... 5.3.4 Apps that offer real money gaming (e.g. sports betting, poker, casino games, horse racing) or lotteries must have necessary licensing and permissions in the locations where the App is used, must be geo-restricted to those locations, and must be free on the App Store. Illegal gambling aids, including card counters, are not permitted on the App Store. Lottery apps must have consideration, chance, and a prize.
notes: The guideline text quoted here is itself a direct quotation of Apple's official guideline as reproduced on Apple's own developer forum; it does not carve out a separate, lighter-touch category for 'skill games' distinct from 'real money gaming' generally — any P2P skill-game cash app (Toast/Carnival, Skillz, Papaya, Triumph) must clear the same 5.3.4 bar as a licensed casino app for the real-money-gaming portions of its product, per Apple's own text. claim: India's Promotion and Regulation of Online Gaming Act, 2025 received presidential assent on Aug 22, 2025 (passed by both chambers Aug 20-21), outlawing all online real-money games nationwide — including formats previously judicially recognized as skill-based (poker, rummy, real-money fantasy sports) — while explicitly carving out esports and non-monetary social/casual gaming as permissible. Implementation rules were notified April 22, 2026; the Act faces constitutional challenges before the Supreme Court of India.
sourceName: Wikipedia — "Promotion and Regulation of Online Gaming Act, 2025"
quote: Passed by Lok Sabha Passed 20 August 2025 ... Passed by Rajya Sabha Passed 21 August 2025 ... Assented to by President of India Assented to 22 August 2025 ... Summary: Creates a regulatory framework for online games in India, including online games, e-sports and online gambling. It prohibits online money games and defines punishment for offering & advertising them. It also recognises e-sports as competitive sport and aims to promote and develop them. ... Classification of Games: Games are classified as: Permissible online games of skill, such as esports and fantasy sports. Prohibited online games of chance, including those involving betting or wagering with money. Hybrid games containing elements of both skill and chance are to be classified by the NOGC. ... On 22 April 2026, the government of India has notified the rules. ... The provisions of the Act have been challenged before Karnataka, Madhya Pradesh and Delhi high courts ... the central government's petition, Supreme Court of India transferred all the pending and future challenge cases to the Supreme Court.
notes: Directly confirms and dates the SOURCES.md research lead: 'Aug 2025: India's Promotion & Regulation of Online Gaming Act reportedly BANNED online real-money games.' Verification nuance found this pass: the page's own "Classification of Games" section frames the skill/chance distinction as governing which games are 'permissible' in the abstract (and even lists "fantasy sports" as an example of a permissible skill game) — but the page's own plain-language Summary states the Act "prohibits online money games" as the operative, broader category, i.e. the real dividing line enforced in practice is money vs. no-money, not skill vs. chance. That reading is what's actually borne out on the ground: Dream11 paused ALL paid contests (wsc-india-rmg-ban-impact-dream11) and WinZO's founders were arrested for continuing REAL-MONEY games post-ban (wsc-winzo-founders-arrested-2025), even though both offer/offered games long characterized as 'skill-based' in Indian courts. Confidence in the ban's practical scope remains high; the skill-vs-chance classification language should be read as secondary to the money-game prohibition.

claim: Toast is NOT the only entrant combining real-money, real-time, peer-to-peer blackjack; the field splits by format and stakes. (1) The large consumer 'skill blackjack' category (Blackout Blackjack, 21 Blitz, 21 Jack, Blackjack Duel) is casual-stakes, ASYNCHRONOUS same-deck solitaire-blackjack hybrids, mostly Skillz-powered, not casino-rules live head-to-head. (2) An early / pre-launch entrant off the original roster, bjb.gg ('Blackjack Battles'), markets exactly this model — real-time, peer-to-peer, real-money casino-rules blackjack with an explicit 'no house edge', on crypto (AVAX) rails and with a bot fallback — evidence the space is beginning to draw entrants, though it is not confirmed to be operating at scale. (3) Thndr's 'Clinch' offers PvP skill-blackjack as B2B infrastructure over Bitcoin Lightning. (4) The casino-stakes real-time names in the researched roster (VGW, Stake.us) are house-banked sweepstakes, a different legal workaround. Net: the peer-to-peer-blackjack CONCEPT is not unique to Toast. Its remaining, still-uncontested differentiators are casino-LEVEL FIAT stakes (USD, ~$50-100+/hand) delivered as an iOS-native consumer app, plus its paid offshore human-counterparty liquidity model. The '2x2 empty quadrant' is more accurately read as thinly populated and sensitive to where the stakes/format lines are drawn, with early crypto-native (bjb.gg) and B2B (Thndr) entrants beginning to move into the casino-rules real-time P2P cell.
sourceName: This workstream's synthesis across all fetched roster sources (see wsc-skillz-solitaire-cube-realtime-matching, wsc-triumph-realtime-live-multiplayer, wsc-prizepicks-arena-mechanics-async, wsc-stakeus-operator-easygo, wsc-papaya-games-portfolio, wsc-skillz-q2-2021-revenue-guidance)
methodology: RECOMPUTED this pass. Cross-referenced the business-model/game-mechanics fact for every roster company gathered in this workstream (see 'notes' field and linked fact IDs) against two axes — stakes level (casual $0.10-$10+ vs. casino $50-100+) and matching model (async/tournament-score-compare vs. genuinely live/real-time synchronous) — using each company's own disclosed game mechanics and entry-fee ranges as evidence. Original pass treated Skillz's Solitaire Cube and Triumph's 1v1 mode as confirmed real-time/synchronous examples of casual-stakes P2P play. This is an analytical synthesis fact (the 2x2 map placement itself), not a single-source factual claim, hence tier D with this methodology note; it does NOT independently verify Toast/Carnival's own claimed mechanics (that is WS-A's task) — it only establishes that no OTHER researched competitor credibly occupies the casino-stakes quadrant, real-time or otherwise. claim: The federal Unlawful Internet Gambling Enforcement Act (31 U.S.C. §5362) excludes fantasy/simulation sports contests from the definition of 'bet or wager' only if: (1) prizes are disclosed in advance and not funded solely from entry fees; (2) winning outcomes reflect the relative knowledge/skill of participants and are determined by accumulated statistical results across multiple real-world events; and (3) no team is based on an actual real-world team's current roster and no outcome turns on a single team's score or a single athlete's single-event performance.
sourceName: Cornell Law School Legal Information Institute — 31 U.S. Code §5362 (Definitions)
quote: (ix) participation in any fantasy or simulation sports game or educational game or contest in which (if the game or contest involves a team or teams) no fantasy or simulation sports team is based on the current membership of an actual team that is a member of an amateur or professional sports organization ... and that meets the following conditions: (I) All prizes and awards offered to winning participants are established and made known to the participants in advance of the game or contest and their value is not determined by the number of participants or the amount of any fees paid by those participants. (II) All winning outcomes reflect the relative knowledge and skill of the participants and are determined predominantly by accumulated statistical results of the performance of individuals (athletes in the case of sports events) in multiple real-world sporting or other events. (III) No winning outcome is based— (aa) on the score, point-spread, or any performance or performances of any single real-world team or any combination of such teams; or (bb) solely on any single performance of an individual athlete in any single real-world sporting or other event.
notes: This is the federal skill-game carve-out language SOURCES.md asks to be pulled verbatim. Note it is narrowly scoped to fantasy/simulation sports specifically, not to skill-based casino-style games like blackjack/craps — the broader 'skill vs. chance' doctrine enabling Toast/Skillz/Papaya/Triumph operates instead through state-level common law and statutes (WS-D's territory), not this federal fantasy-sports-specific carve-out.
