The flagship gaming app hosts live trivia quiz tournaments and has seen over 250 million game plays. (Article dated 2021-08-18.)

claim: Realistic addressable market for P2P cash blackjack is roughly 32-37 states — not the ~43 states without iCasino implied by the pitch; the operator-consensus red zone plus card-game statutes and hostile-doctrine states remove 14-19 jurisdictions.
sourceName: WS-D synthesis of operator T&Cs, statutes, and doctrine surveys
methodology: Start from 50 states + DC. Step 1 — operator-consensus red zone (excluded for ALL cash play by >=2 of the 5 list-publishing operators — Skillz, AviaGames, Papaya, Triumph, Carnival — each backed by statute or enforcement): LA (5/5), AR (3), DE (3), SD (3), SC (3, incl. Carnival), MI (3, incl. Carnival), CT (2), AZ (3, incl. Carnival & Papaya) -> 43 jurisdictions where cash skill games are broadly offered. Step 2 — blackjack is a card game: subtract IN and ME (per-se card statutes; card exclusions by Skillz, AviaGames and Triumph) -> 41; subtract the remaining card-exclusion states IA (Carnival + Papaya full cash-exclusions + Triumph cards + any-chance case law), OR, PA, WA (Triumph cards; WA felony statute) -> 37 optimistic. Step 3 — subtract states where a fetched authority is hostile to mixed skill-chance wagering: FL, MT, TN (Triumph-excluded; wager-based/online-ban/any-chance authorities), TX (any-chance per practitioner survey, no operator exclusion), and CA (Penal Code 330 names twenty-one; predominance state otherwise) -> 32 conservative. Range: 32 (conservative) to 37 (optimistic). Cross-check 1: Skillz claims 40 US states for real-prize competitions generally (non-card skill games), bounding the ceiling. Cross-check 2: Carnival's own consumer T&Cs already concede 5 states (AZ, SC, MI, IA, LA -> 46 by Toast's own map), before the AR/CT/DE/SD/TN exposures its peers price in.
notes: Tier D estimate, revised after adding Carnival's consumer T&C list (wsd-carnival-consumer-tos-excluded-states): AZ moved into the >=2-operator red zone, so the optimistic bound tightened from 38 to 37; the conservative bound is unchanged at 32 (same total removed set). The gap between '~43 states without iCasino' and '~32-37 blackjack-addressable' is the single most important correction to the diligence narrative. Counts treat DC as a jurisdiction (Triumph supports DC). Re-verified 2026-07-14 (verify-wsd): arithmetic recomputed 51 - 8 red-zone - 2 card-statute (IN, ME) - 4 peer-card (IA, OR, PA, WA) - 5 hostile (FL, MT, TN, TX, CA) = 32; upper bound 37 (before Step 3) — confirmed. The Papaya T&C re-read (see wsd-papaya-tos-prohibited-states) STRENGTHENED the red zone: AZ is now excluded by 3 operators (Triumph, Carnival, Papaya) not 2, and Papaya fully prohibits IA for cash — but both were already subtracted, so the 32-37 range is unchanged.

claim: Arkansas criminalizes betting on 'any game of hazard or skill' — skill classification is no defense; wagering itself is the offense.
sourceName: Arkansas Code § 5-66-113 (via FindLaw)
quote: If a person bets any money or any valuable thing on any game of hazard or skill, upon conviction he or she is guilty of a violation and shall be fined in any sum not less than ten dollars ($10.00) nor more than twenty-five dollars ($25.00).
notes: Explains why Arkansas is excluded by Skillz, AviaGames and Triumph. Edelman (wsd-edelman-any-chance-states) also groups Arkansas with the strictest any-chance states.

claim: AviaGames' Terms of Service additionally exclude card game cash competitions in Indiana and Maine.
sourceName: AviaGames — Terms of Service
quote: Aviagames DOES NOT offer card game Cash Competitions if you reside in, or access the Apps from, the U.S. States of Indiana or Maine.
notes: Section 2.8. Identical card-game carve-out states as Skillz (wsd-skillz-tos-card-exclusions) — a strong industry consensus signal on card games.

claim: AviaGames' (Pocket7Games) Terms of Service, last updated August 18, 2025, prohibit cash competitions in Arkansas, Delaware, Louisiana, South Carolina, and South Dakota, plus US territories including Puerto Rico.
sourceName: AviaGames — Terms of Service
quote: Aviagames DOES NOT offer games requiring a cash entry fee ('Cash Competitions') to you if you access the Services from the U.S. States of Arkansas, Delaware, Louisiana, South Carolina or South Dakota or from U.S. territories including Puerto Rico.
notes: Section 2.6. Document also states: 'SERVICES AND COMPETITIONS ARE VOID WHERE PROHIBITED OR RESTRICTED BY APPLICABLE LAWS.'

claim: Gaming-law commentary uses blackjack as the canonical example of a mixed skill-chance game that fails the any-chance test: card flips are chance even though hit/stand decisions are skill.
sourceName: Walters Law Group — Skill Gaming Legal Guide
quote: For example, the game of Blackjack involves chance card flips and the skill of the player in deciding whether to "hit" or "stand." Since this game involves at least some chance (the card flips), it likely would not pass the Any Chance test and would be considered gambling.
notes: No fetched source identifies any US court holding blackjack skill-predominant; the affirmative authorities cut the other way (IN per-se card statute, ME skill-elimination test, CA § 330 naming twenty-one, NY material degree). Toast's symmetric-deal PvP format is a genuinely novel, untested legal theory.

claim: California Penal Code § 330 criminalizes dealing or playing 'twenty-one' by name, among enumerated 'banking or percentage games' played with cards for money — regardless of any skill analysis.
sourceName: California Penal Code § 330 (leginfo.legislature.ca.gov)
quote: faro, monte, roulette, lansquenet, rouge et noire, rondo, tan, fan-tan, seven-and-a-half, twenty-one, hokey-pokey
notes: The statute reaches 'any banking or percentage game played with cards, dice, or any device, for money.' Toast's peer-to-peer 'no dealer or RNG edge' design (wsd-playtoast-21-hustle-no-dealer) is an untested argument that its 21 is neither banked nor a percentage game; California is otherwise a predominance state (wsd-doctrine-three-tests-states). The illustrative example was blackjack-on-a-phone in California.

claim: Toast's consumer app is listed on the Apple App Store as 'Carnival Hustle: 21,Basketball' by Toast Studios, Inc., marketed as a skill-based cash-competition platform; the listing's description contains no excluded-states disclosure.
sourceName: Apple iTunes Lookup API — app id 6745267121
quote: Welcome to Carnival — the ultimate skill-based gaming platform where your ability decides your rewards. Challenge real players in live 1v1 matches and earn real cash for every win — fast, fair, and full of adrenaline.
notes: Machine-readable Apple metadata. Competitor app listings (e.g., Pocket7Games) typically disclose excluded states in the description; Carnival's does not — its exclusion list lives only in the consumer T&Cs at carnivalplay.com (wsd-carnival-consumer-tos-excluded-states). Disambiguation confirmed: developer 'Toast Studios, Inc.' — not Toast POS (NYSE: TOST), not Carnival Cruise Line.

claim: Carnival's consumer-app Terms of Use (Toast Studios, Inc., last updated May 10, 2025, at carnivalplay.com) exclude five US states from cash tournaments — Arizona, South Carolina, Michigan, Iowa, Louisiana — and describe all competitions as games of skill, with restricted-state users limited to free practice modes via geolocation.
sourceName: Carnival (carnivalplay.com) — Terms of Service, Eligible Locations
quote: Arizona, South Carolina, Michigan, Iowa, Louisiana
notes: Fetched and confirmed independently 2026-07-14 (matches WS-A fact wsa-excluded-states-carnival, section 2.3 Eligible Locations). Also verbatim on page: 'All competitions offered on Carnival are games of skill, not games of chance' and 'You may only access the paid contest features of Carnival if you are physically located in a jurisdiction where skill-based competitions for prizes are allowed and not restricted by law.' Analytical read: the five exclusions are card/blackjack-aware picks (AZ+IA any-chance, SC cards/dice statute, MI MGCB enforcement, LA universal no) — but the list is notably SHORT vs peers and omits AR (betting on games 'of hazard or skill' criminalized), CT, DE, SD (each excluded by 2-3 peers) and TN (any-chance). Carnival appears to be under-excluding hostile states relative to operator consensus.

claim: Connecticut defines gambling as risking anything of value 'for gain contingent in whole or in part upon lot, chance or the operation of a gambling device' — an in-part-chance standard hostile to mixed skill-chance games.
sourceName: Conn. Gen. Stat. § 53-278a (via FindLaw)
quote: risking any money, credit, deposit or other thing of value for gain contingent in whole or in part upon lot, chance or the operation of a gambling device
notes: The definition excludes 'legal contests of skill' — making everything turn on whether a given game qualifies. Skillz and Triumph exclude CT; AviaGames and Papaya serve it. CT also runs a regulated iCasino duopoly and banned sweepstakes casinos in June 2025 (wsd-sweeps-crackdown-2025).

claim: Most states have adopted the dominant factor (predominance) test; other states follow the material element test; a few adhere to the any-chance test under which any chance influence makes a game illegal gambling.
sourceName: Klein Moynihan Turco LLP — Games of Skill v. Games of Chance: The Legal Analysis
quote: Most states have adopted the dominant factor test, also known as the predominance test, to analyze whether a game is one of skill or chance.
notes: On NY: the legislature defined gambling as 'any contest, game, gaming scheme or gaming device in which the outcome depends in a material degree upon any element of chance, notwithstanding that skill of the contestants may also be a factor therein.' Also: 'a few states adhere to the "any chance" test, which dictates that if chance influences the outcome of a game in any way, it will be deemed illegal gambling.'

claim: More than 30 states (including CA, GA, IL, MI, NC, OH, PA) use the predominance test; some states (including NY) use the material element test; a remaining few (including TX) apply an 'any chance' / gambling-instinct standard.
sourceName: California Gaming Lawyer (Kane) — What determines if a game is one of Skill or Chance?
quote: More than 30 states (including California, Georgia, Illinois, Michigan, North Carolina, Ohio and Pennsylvania) ask whether skill 'predominates' over chance
notes: Also: "Some states (including New York) look to whether chance is a 'material element'" and "The remaining few states (including Texas) will regard 'any chance' being involved or the notion that the game appeals to gambling instincts sufficient to trigger gambling prohibitions." Gaming-law practitioner survey; state-level assignments beyond those named are NOT provided by this source.

claim: Arizona, Arkansas, Iowa, Louisiana, and Tennessee have interpreted contests as illegal games of chance if they involve any chance at all, even a modicum of chance.
sourceName: Marc Edelman, Navigating the Legal Risks of Daily Fantasy Sports, 2016 U. Ill. L. Rev. 117
quote: Even stricter still, states such as Arizona, Arkansas, Iowa, Louisiana, and Tennessee have interpreted contests to be illegal games of chance if they involve any chance at all, even a modicum of chance.
notes: Page 134-35. 2016 law review; doctrines are stable but check for post-2016 carve-outs (e.g., AZ softened by State v. American Holiday Ass'n per fn 105; TN later enacted fantasy/sports carve-outs). PDF read directly (pp. 128-141).

claim: Arizona's gambling definition covers risking value on 'a game or contest of chance or skill'; Tennessee's covers profit 'to any degree contingent on chance'; Iowa case law finds it irrelevant whether a game is predominantly chance or skill.
sourceName: Marc Edelman, Navigating the Legal Risks of Daily Fantasy Sports, 2016 U. Ill. L. Rev. 117 (fn 105)
quote: ARIZ. REV. STAT. § 13-3301(4) (2014) (defining gambling as 'risking or giving something of value for the opportunity to obtain a benefit from a game or contest of chance or skill or a future contingent event')
notes: Same footnote: TENN. CODE ANN. § 39-17-501 defines gambling as 'risking anything of value for a profit whose return is to any degree contingent on chance'; Parker-Gordon Importing Co. v. Benakis, 238 N.W. 611, 613 (Iowa 1931) ('Iowa finds it irrelevant whether a particular game is predominantly based on chance or skill'); but State v. Am. Holiday Ass'n, 727 P.2d 807, 809-11 (Ariz. 1986) softened Arizona's rule for entrance-fee contests decided by entrants' 'own mental or physical abilities.' Statute text quoted secondhand via law review — statutes themselves not fetched.

claim: Hawaii forbids contests that encourage a 'gambling instinct' (State v. Prevo, 1961) — an idiosyncratic test creating ambiguity for pay-to-play skill contests.
sourceName: Marc Edelman, Navigating the Legal Risks of Daily Fantasy Sports, 2016 U. Ill. L. Rev. 117
quote: Meanwhile, states such as Hawaii forbid contests that encourage a gambling instinct.
notes: Fn 109: State v. Prevo, 361 P.2d 1044, 1050-51 (Haw. 1961). All four operators nonetheless serve Hawaii.

claim: Missouri and New York apply a material element test, under which a contest can be illegal even if mathematically 51%+ skill.
sourceName: Marc Edelman, Navigating the Legal Risks of Daily Fantasy Sports, 2016 U. Ill. L. Rev. 117
quote: Among these many stricter tests for skill, states such as Missouri and New York apply a 'material element test.'
notes: 'Under the material element test, it is possible for a court to find a contest is not determined by skill even if one can prove mathematically that the contest is 51% or more skill.' (p. 134, citing Thole v. Westfall (Mo. Ct. App. 1984) and US v. DiCristina (2d Cir. 2013)).

claim: Montana (and Louisiana) disallow all forms of commercial online gambling irrespective of whether the underlying contest is skill or chance.
sourceName: Marc Edelman, Navigating the Legal Risks of Daily Fantasy Sports, 2016 U. Ill. L. Rev. 117
quote: Finally, states such as Montana disallow all forms of commercial online gambling, irrespective of whether the underlying contest would be deemed a game of skill or chance.
notes: Fn 108 cites Mont. Code Ann. § 23-5-112 and describes 'Louisiana as another such state that disallows all forms of Internet gaming.' Skillz/Avia/Papaya nonetheless serve Montana today; only Triumph excludes it — flagged contested.

claim: A majority of states adopt the 'predominant purpose test' as the measure of chance; New Jersey applies it per O'Brien v. Scott — the test is which element dominates the result of the game.
sourceName: Marc Edelman, Navigating the Legal Risks of Daily Fantasy Sports, 2016 U. Ill. L. Rev. 117
quote: A majority of states adopt . . . the 'predominant purpose test' as the measure of chance.
notes: Fn 74, quoting Edelman's A Short Treatise; cites O'Brien v. Scott, 89 A.2d 280, 283 (N.J. Super. Ct. Ch. Div. 1952): '[t]he test of the character of the game is ... which is the dominating element that determines the result of the game.'

claim: The eight states with legal online casinos as of July 2026 are Connecticut, Delaware, Maine, Michigan, New Jersey, Pennsylvania, Rhode Island, and West Virginia (Maine legal but not yet launched).
value: CT, DE, ME, MI, NJ, PA, RI, WV
sourceName: SportsLine (CBS Sports) — Legal Online Casinos in the USA
quote: Eight states currently offer legal online casinos, including Connecticut, Delaware, Maine, Michigan, New Jersey, Pennsylvania, Rhode Island, and West Virginia.
notes: Same page: 'Maine became the eighth and newest state to enter the online casino USA legal market in 2026. It has yet to launch its online casinos.' Cross-checked against wsd-icasino-seven-live-2025 (AGA: 7 live in 2025) and wsd-maine-ld1164-became-law (tier A).

claim: US iGaming (online casino) revenue was $10.73 billion in 2025 across the seven live states; total US commercial gaming revenue was a record $78.62 billion, up 9.1% over 2024.
sourceName: Casino Reports — AGA State of the States 2026 coverage
quote: the $10.73 billion in iGaming revenue they generated was an increase of more than $2 billion over the previous year. ... Total U.S. commercial gaming revenue for 2025 came in at a record $78.62 billion, up 9.1% over the 2024 figure

claim: Only seven states had active regulated online casino (iCasino) markets in 2025, per the AGA's State of the States 2026 analysis.
sourceName: Casino Reports — AGA State of the States 2026 coverage
quote: Though there were only seven active regulated online casino states last year (with Maine set to become the eighth, possibly before the end of 2026)
notes: AGA's own site (americangaming.org) returned HTTP 403 to our fetcher; Casino Reports summarizes the AGA State of the States 2026 report. The seven live states are CT, DE, MI, NJ, PA, RI, WV (see wsd-icasino-eight-states-list).

claim: Ifrah Law identifies South Carolina, Maryland, and Michigan as outlier states whose laws focus on whether a wager is placed (disregarding the skill-chance distinction), and Washington as prohibiting online gaming via case law and enforcement despite skill-friendly-looking statutory text.
sourceName: Ifrah Law — Gambling on Skill: Enforcement of Gambling Laws Against Skill Games in Outlier States
quote: in South Carolina, recording bets or selling pools on contests of skill is prohibited.
notes: SC Supreme Court: 'gambling depends not on skill or chance, but rather on whether a wager is involved.' MD and MI laws 'each focus on whether a wager is placed and disregard the chance-skill distinction used by the majority.' WA 'may seem to allow games of skill based on statutory language, but the case law and regulatory enforcement actually prohibit any online gaming.' Enforcement example: SC Court of Appeals ruled against the Dragon's Ascent skill video game (2025).

claim: Indiana's gambling statute declares by statute that a card game or an electronic version of a card game IS a game of chance and may not be considered a bona fide contest of skill — a categorical bar to cash blackjack.
sourceName: Indiana Code § 35-45-5-1 (via FindLaw)
quote: a card game; or an electronic version of a card game; is a game of chance and may not be considered a bona fide contest of skill.
notes: Gambling defined as 'risking money or other property for gain, contingent in whole or in part upon lot, chance, or the operation of a gambling device.' This is why Skillz, AviaGames and Triumph all exclude card games in Indiana while serving the state for non-card games. No skill argument — however sophisticated the matching — survives a per-se statute.

claim: Louisiana has a dedicated 'gambling by computer' crime (La. R.S. 14:90.3) reaching any business conducted over the internet 'whereby a person risks the loss of anything of value in order to realize a profit', with up to 5 years for operators/providers.
sourceName: La. R.S. 14:90.3 (via FindLaw)
quote: Gambling by computer is the intentional conducting, or directly assisting in the conducting as a business of any game, contest, lottery, or contrivance whereby a person risks the loss of anything of value in order to realize a profit when accessing the Internet
notes: Louisiana is the ONLY state excluded by all FIVE list-publishing operators (Skillz, AviaGames, Papaya, Triumph, and Carnival per wsd-carnival-consumer-tos-excluded-states). Operator/infrastructure liability: 'fined not more than twenty thousand dollars, or imprisoned with or without hard labor, for not more than five years, or both.'

claim: Maine's LD 1164 became law in January 2026, authorizing the four Wabanaki Nations tribal governments to operate online gambling (iGaming) in Maine — making Maine the eighth state to legalize online casino gaming.
sourceName: Office of Governor Janet T. Mills — press release
quote: LD 1164, An Act to Create Economic Opportunity for the Wabanaki Nations ... authorizes Maine's tribal governments to operate online gambling in Maine to support tribal communities and economic development.
notes: Governor Mills allowed the bill to become law without signature: 'I considered this bill carefully, and while I have concerns about the impacts of gambling on public health, I believe that this new form of gambling should be regulated.' Launch expected ~early 2027 per trade press.

claim: Maine defines a 'contest of chance' as one where chance 'enters as an element that influences the outcome in a manner that can not be eliminated through the application of skill' — card draws in blackjack cannot be eliminated by skill, so card games fail.
sourceName: 17-A M.R.S. § 952 (via FindLaw)
quote: Chance enters as an element that influences the outcome in a manner that can not be eliminated through the application of skill.
notes: A skill-elimination test, stricter than material element. Explains the identical Skillz/AviaGames card-game exclusions for Maine (wsd-skillz-tos-card-exclusions, wsd-avia-tos-card-exclusions); Triumph excludes Maine entirely. Maine is simultaneously becoming an iCasino state (wsd-maine-ld1164-became-law).

claim: The Michigan Gaming Control Board issued a cease-and-desist letter to Papaya Gaming (Oct 2024) over 21 Cash, Bingo Cash, Bubble Cash, and Solitaire Cash, treating cash-entry skill games as illegal unlicensed gambling under three Michigan statutes.
sourceName: Michigan Gaming Control Board — news release (GovDelivery)
quote: Papaya Gaming has 14 days from receipt of the letter to take steps to prevent Michigan residents from gambling on their apps or the MGCB will take legal action
notes: Apps named: '21 Cash, Bingo Cash, Bubble Cash, and Solitaire Cash.' Laws cited: Lawful Internet Gaming Act ('internet gaming may only be offered by a licensed internet gaming operator'), Michigan Gaming Control and Revenue Act, and Michigan Penal Code ('broadly prohibits any form of unauthorized gambling involving consideration, prize, and chance'). 21 Cash is BLACKJACK — the single most on-point US enforcement action for Toast's model. Papaya's ToS now exclude Michigan (wsd-papaya-tos-prohibited-states); the michigan.gov mirror of this release returned 403 to our fetcher, GovDelivery bulletin is the same official text.

claim: Nevada permits online poker but not online casino games; it is not an iCasino state.
sourceName: SportsLine (CBS Sports) — Legal Online Casinos in the USA
quote: Nevada is another of the states where online gambling is legal, but only for iPoker, not casinos.

claim: New York defines a 'contest of chance' as any game in which the outcome 'depends in a material degree upon an element of chance' even if skill is also a factor — the canonical material element test.
sourceName: NY Penal Law § 225.00 (NY Senate)
quote: any contest, game, gaming scheme or gaming device in which the outcome depends in a material degree upon an element of chance
notes: Gambling: 'A person engages in gambling when he stakes or risks something of value upon the outcome of a contest of chance or a future contingent event not under his control or influence...' Blackjack's card draw is hard to argue below 'material degree.' Task-13 link-check note: nysenate.gov intermittently 403s this checker's plain User-Agent (bot-wall, same family as the sec.gov/macrotrends issues); an existing Wayback snapshot (2026-01-21) was found via the archive.org availability API and added as archiveUrl so the gate resolves deterministically.

claim: Papaya Gaming's terms-of-use page aggregates separate Terms for multiple products, each with a DIFFERENT US prohibited-jurisdiction list; the real-money Cash Competition terms (most recent explicit list eff. June 9, 2025) prohibit Iowa outright and restrict Arizona, Louisiana, Michigan (all games) and Maine (card games) to non-redeemable 'Bonus Cash' play — NOT the two-state (LA, MI) list, which appears only in the separate free 'Solitaire Town Tycoon' sweepstakes section. The most restrictive product version (eff. Feb 11, 2025) additionally lists South Carolina as prohibited and Indiana and Montana for card-based games.
value: IA prohibited; AZ, LA, MI bonus-cash-only; ME cards; restrictive version adds SC (prohibited), IN, MT (cards)
sourceName: Papaya Gaming — Terms of Use
quote: In the United States, Prohibited Jurisdictions include: Iowa. Users located in Arizona, Louisiana, Michigan and Maine (for card-based games; collectively "Bonus Cash States") may pay to participate in skill-based competitions, but all competitions for Bonus Cash States-based users are only for fun, and award prizes in an in-game currency called “Bonus Cash” that cannot be redeemed for real money.
notes: CORRECTED 2026-07-14 (verify-wsd): the prior 'LA, MI only' claim was an extraction-placement error and is retracted. papaya.com/terms-of-use is ONE page aggregating many product/version Terms, each with its own prohibited-jurisdiction list. The 'Louisiana, and Michigan' line is from the free/sweepstakes 'Solitaire Town Tycoon' Tournaments section (eff. 3 Sep 2025), NOT the real-money Cash Competition terms. Real-money Cash Competition lists on the page: eff. 9 Jun 2025 / 17 Apr 2025 / 21 Apr 2025 -> prohibited: Iowa; Bonus-Cash-only (entry allowed, prizes non-redeemable): Arizona, Louisiana, Michigan; card-based add: Maine. Eff. 26 Mar 2025 -> prohibited: Arizona, Iowa; Bonus-Cash: Louisiana, Michigan; cards: Maine. Eff. 11 Feb 2025 (most restrictive) -> prohibited: Arizona, Iowa, Louisiana, South Carolina; card-based add: Indiana, Maine, Montana; Bonus-Cash: Michigan. Sweepstakes Tournament sections: LA+MI (3 Sep 2025), AZ+LA+MI (7 Jul 2025), LA+MI+MT (12 Feb 2026). The NEWEST terms (Google Play, eff. 27 Jan 2026, covering Solitaire Cash/Bingo Cash/Bubble Cash) dropped the explicit state list for generic geo-blocking language. Michigan's restriction follows the MGCB C&D (wsd-mgcb-papaya-cease-desist). Net effect for the state matrix: Papaya effectively offers NO redeemable-cash play in IA, AZ, LA, MI (and ME/IN/MT for card games) -> see states.json repairs to IA (excludedByOperators 1->2) and AZ (2->3). 21 Cash (blackjack) is a card game, so the card-based carve-outs apply to Toast's model.

claim: Toast markets '21 Hustle (PvP Blackjack)' as head-to-head blackjack with no dealer or RNG edge — an explicit design attempt to avoid house-banked game status.
sourceName: Toast (playtoast.com) — homepage games list
quote: 21 Hustle (PvP Blackjack) — Head-to-head blackjack with no dealer or RNG edge
notes: Other games listed: Basketball Hustle, Pinfall, Fling the Piggy, Easy Bingo, Gin Rummy, Cricket PowerPlay, Pinfall Manic — matching the site-games observation (ws0-site-games-list-playtoast). 'No dealer or RNG edge' is aimed at statutes like CA Penal Code 330 that prohibit 'banking or percentage games' (wsd-ca-penal-330-twenty-one).

claim: South Carolina criminalizes playing or betting at 'any game with cards or dice' in gaming houses — a per-se card/dice prohibition indifferent to skill.
sourceName: S.C. Code § 16-19-40 (scstatehouse.gov)
quote: play at any tavern, inn, store for the retailing of spirituous liquors or in any house used as a place of gaming ... at (a) any game with cards or dice
notes: Also unlawful to 'bet on the sides or hands of such as do game.' AviaGames and Triumph exclude SC entirely; Skillz and Papaya currently serve it — a genuine conflict. See wsd-ifrah-outlier-states for the SC Supreme Court's wager-focused test and the 2025 Dragon's Ascent appellate ruling.

claim: Skillz states it powers real-prize competitions in roughly 80% of the world and 40 US states.
sourceName: Skillz Developer Docs — The Legality of Skill Gaming
quote: powers real prize competitions in roughly 80% of the world and 40 US states
notes: Operator's own claim about itself (tier A as a statement of Skillz's position). Inconsistent with the current ToS which lists only 5 fully prohibited states (would imply 45): the 40-state figure likely reflects an older, longer exclusion list or game-level restrictions. Notably close to the '40 states' framing.

claim: Skillz's legal explainer characterizes the material element test as used by 8 states, alongside the predominance test as the most common standard.
sourceName: Skillz Developer Docs — The Legality of Skill Gaming
quote: Material Element Test (used by 8 states): This evaluates whether chance plays a material role in determining a game's outcome.
notes: Skillz is an interested party characterizing the legal landscape; the doc does not name the 8 states. Treated as tier B legal commentary, not operator fact.

claim: Skillz's Terms additionally prohibit cash competitions for card games in Maine and Indiana, and for Dominoes Gold in New Jersey — treating card games as a legally distinct, riskier category.
sourceName: Skillz — User Terms and Conditions of Service (skillz.com/legal)
quote: For card games, Prohibited Jurisdictions include Maine and Indiana. For Dominoes Gold, Prohibited Jurisdictions include New Jersey.

claim: Skillz's User Terms of Service prohibit cash competitions in five US states: Arkansas, Connecticut, Delaware, Louisiana, and South Dakota.
sourceName: Skillz — User Terms and Conditions of Service (skillz.com/legal)
quote: In the United States, Prohibited Jurisdictions, as of the 'Updated' date above, include: Arkansas, Connecticut, Delaware, Louisiana, and South Dakota.
notes: Eligibility also requires being 'physically located within a U.S. state in which participation in the Competition you select is unrestricted.' Note internal inconsistency with Skillz's marketing/legal explainer claiming 40 US states (wsd-skillz-40-states-claim).

claim: In 2025 New York (SB 5935), California (AB 831), Connecticut, Montana and New Jersey banned sweepstakes casinos; the NY AG sent 26 cease-and-desist letters, the Louisiana Gaming Control Board sent 40, and Arizona and Michigan contributed to 100+ more — the cautionary parallel for gray-market gaming models.
sourceName: iGaming Business — How 2025 became the year US states turned against sweepstakes casinos
quote: New York Governor Kathy Hochul signed into law Senate Bill 5935 to ban sweepstakes casinos.
notes: Also verbatim from the article: 'California Governor Gavin Newsom...signed Assembly Bill 831 in October. AB 831 passed both California legislative chambers unanimously'; 'Along with New York and California, lawmakers in Connecticut, Montana and New Jersey passed similar legislation'; NY AG 'sent cease-and-desist letters to 26 sweepstakes operators'; 'The Louisiana Gaming Control Board then sent 40 cease-and-desist letters to unregulated gambling operators'; 'Other states like Arizona and Michigan also contributed in sending more than 100 cease-and-desist letters.' Lesson: when a workaround model scales, states legislate and enforce within ~2 years.

claim: Toast's B2B/platform-site Terms of Service (Toast Studio, Inc., effective 09/26/2025, at playtoast.com/terms) contain no excluded-states list and no eligibility/age/location section — the state exclusion list for the consumer product lives in the separate Carnival app Terms at carnivalplay.com (see wsd-carnival-consumer-tos-excluded-states).
sourceName: Toast (playtoast.com) — Terms of Service
quote: Toast Studio, Inc. ... Effective Date: 09/26/2025
methodology: Full page fetched and read twice (2026-07-14) with prompts specifically hunting for eligibility, jurisdiction, state-exclusion, and compliance-with-laws sections; none exist in THIS document. Scope limitation: this finding applies to the playtoast.com platform ToS only — the Carnival consumer app publishes its own Terms of Use at carnivalplay.com/terms-of-service/ with an Eligible Locations section (fetched and confirmed 2026-07-14, cross-consistent with WS-A fact wsa-excluded-states-carnival).
notes: Absence finding scoped to the platform-site document; do NOT read as 'no list exists anywhere.' The quote is the only jurisdiction-relevant text on the playtoast.com page (entity + effective date). Residual observation: the B2B site's ToS and the App Store description are both silent on states; geographic eligibility is disclosed only in the consumer T&C document.

claim: Triumph excludes all card-based games in Indiana, Iowa, Oregon, Pennsylvania, South Carolina, and Washington; Bingo in AL, WA, OR, IL, NE, NC, SC, OH; and Super Peg Master in DC, HI, MO, NJ, NM, NY, TX, WA.
value: cards: IN, IA, OR, PA, SC, WA
sourceName: Triumph Help Center — Game Exclusions by State
quote: All Card-Based Games excluded in: Indiana, Iowa, Oregon, Pennsylvania, South Carolina, Washington
notes: Bingo excluded in: 'Alabama, Washington, Oregon, Illinois, Nebraska, North Carolina, South Carolina, Ohio'; Super Peg Master excluded in: 'District of Columbia, Hawaii, Missouri, New Jersey, New Mexico, New York, Texas, Washington.' Directly material to blackjack: a card game.

claim: Triumph is supported in only 36 US jurisdictions (35 states plus Washington DC); the 15 excluded states are AZ, AR, CT, DE, FL, KY, LA, ME, MI, MS, MT, NV, SC, SD, and TN.
sourceName: Triumph Help Center — Supported States
quote: Triumph Arcade is only supported in 36 states in the United States of America. ... Alabama, Alaska, California, Colorado, Washington DC, Georgia, Hawaii, Idaho, Illinois, Indiana, Iowa, Kansas, Maryland, Massachusetts, Minnesota, Missouri, Nebraska, New Hampshire, New Jersey, New Mexico, New York, North Carolina, North Dakota, Ohio, Oklahoma, Oregon, Pennsylvania, Rhode Island, Texas, Utah, Vermont, Virginia, West Virginia, Washington, Wisconsin, and Wyoming.
notes: Excluded-state list derived by subtracting the published supported list from 50 states: Arizona, Arkansas, Connecticut, Delaware, Florida, Kentucky, Louisiana, Maine, Michigan, Mississippi, Montana, Nevada, South Carolina, South Dakota, Tennessee. Triumph is the most conservative of the five list-publishing operators (Skillz, AviaGames, Papaya, Triumph, Carnival).

claim: Triumph's Terms of Use (Triumph Arcade, LLC, updated July 10, 2026) restrict real-money tournaments to published 'Eligible Locations', describe its games as bona fide skill contests, and flag Illinois device-forfeiture risk.
sourceName: Triumph Docs — Terms of Use, 'Triumph: Play for Cash'
quote: IN CERTAIN JURISDICTIONS, MORE PARTICULARLY BUT NOT LIMITED TO THE STATE OF ILLINOIS, PLAYING REAL CASH TOURNAMENTS IS ALLOWED BUT THE DEVICE ON WHICH SUCH TOURNAMENTS ARE PLAYED MAY BE SUBJECT TO FORFEITURE.
notes: Also: 'The list of Eligible Locations for each Triumph entity ... is published and kept up to date on the Website and/or in the Triumph App' — the published list is wsd-triumph-supported-36-states. Games described as 'bona fide contests of skill' involving 'player speed, reaction time, memory, dexterity, visual acuity, spatial reasoning, and exercise of judgment.' Related Triumph docs FAQ notes: 'Some states do not allow any card games to be considered as skill-based, others exclude dice games' (docs.triumpharcade.com/frequently-asked-questions-regulatory-and-legal).

claim: UIGEA defines 'bet or wager' as staking something of value on the outcome of a contest of others, a sporting event, or 'a game subject to chance' — leaving genuine skill games outside the federal definition.
sourceName: 31 U.S.C. § 5362 (Cornell LII)
quote: means the staking or risking by any person of something of value upon the outcome of a contest of others, a sporting event, or a game subject to chance
notes: §5362(1)(A). Whether P2P blackjack is 'a game subject to chance' is unresolved; courts have read 'subject to chance' more broadly than state predominance tests.

claim: UIGEA's 'unlawful internet gambling' is entirely derivative of state law — a bet is federally unlawful only where it is 'unlawful under any applicable Federal or State law' where initiated or received; the 50-state patchwork therefore controls.
sourceName: 31 U.S.C. § 5362 (Cornell LII)
quote: means to place, receive, or otherwise knowingly transmit a bet or wager by any means which involves the use, at least in part, of the Internet where such bet or wager is unlawful under any applicable Federal or State law in the State or Tribal lands in which the bet or wager is initiated, received, or otherwise made.
notes: §5362(10)(A). Consequence: one hostile state's law can create federal UIGEA/payment-processing exposure for money flows touching that state.

claim: Washington makes knowingly transmitting or receiving gambling information over the internet a class C felony (RCW 9.46.240), the most aggressive anti-online-gambling statute in the country.
sourceName: RCW 9.46.240 (Washington State Legislature)
quote: Whoever knowingly transmits or receives gambling information by telephone, telegraph, radio, semaphore, the internet, a telecommunications transmission system, or similar means
notes: Class C felony per RCW 9A.20.021. Skillz, AviaGames and Papaya nonetheless serve WA (their ToS exclusion lists omit it) while Triumph excludes all card games there — a live example of operators pricing legal risk differently. See wsd-ifrah-outlier-states for case-law characterization.

claim: Florida prohibits wagering on contests of skill by statute, while permitting skill-based tournaments meeting specific criteria (non-variable, pre-announced prizes).
sourceName: Walters Law Group — Which States Allow Skill Gaming?
quote: prohibits wagering on any contest of skill
notes: Cf. Edelman fn 97 (Faircloth v. Cent. Fla. Fair, Fla. 1967): 'Florida allows individuals to wager on contests of skill in which they participate, but disallows wagering on the activities of skill of third parties' — the participant/spectator line matters and is unsettled for entry-fee P2P formats. Triumph excludes FL entirely; Skillz/Avia/Papaya serve it.

claim: A leading gaming-law firm states that any specific list of states where skill gaming is legal is elusive — three lawyers will give three different lists — underscoring that the 'legal state count' is judgment, not fact.
sourceName: Walters Law Group — Which States Allow Skill Gaming?
quote: any specific list of states where skill gaming is legal is elusive...Ask 3 lawyers for such a list and you may get 3 different lists.
notes: Core epistemic caveat for the whole state matrix: our per-state classifications lean on operator revealed preferences (T&C exclusion lists) plus statutes, and on 'contested' labels where authorities conflict.

claim: Analysis (prosecutor's lens): a prosecutor could argue that because Toast recruits, pays and controls its offshore counterparties, the US player is not meaningfully competing against an independent human but against house-supplied liquidity, making the activity economically equivalent to wagering against the house — the same 'the opponent is not what it appears' theory that generated fraud/illegal-gambling exposure in AviaGames, distinguished only by paid humans vs. bots.
sourceName: Toast WS-E prosecutor analysis
methodology: Reasoning by analogy from the AviaGames bot-fraud theory and grand-jury probe (facts wse-aviagames-bots-controlled, wse-aviagames-doj-grand-jury) and the skill-vs-house legal mechanism (ws0-game-of-skill-vs-dealer-mechanism). Not a prediction of any charge. The strongest defense rebuttal: Toast's counterparties are real humans exercising real skill and bearing real (if small) stakes, so there is no misrepresentation that opponents are human and no bot; the AviaGames/Papaya cases turned on non-human opponents and on affirmative 'we do not use bots' lies, neither of which is present if Toast discloses offshore human matching.
notes: Labeled analysis, tier D. Directly answers the prosecutor-lens question. The load-bearing factual disputes for any real case would be: who bears the economic risk on the counterparty's hand, how counterparties are compensated relative to outcomes, and what users are told.

claim: Analysis: India's 2025 Act bans offering online money games and their financial facilitation (the consumer-wagering/operator side); paying a person a wage/allowance to play is labor rather than staking one's own money for a monetary return, so Toast's paid Indian blackjack workers are not obviously 'players' of an 'online money game' as defined — but Toast or its Indian entity could still be exposed as a 'person enabling' online money games or facilitating related financial transactions, with FEMA/cross-border-pay and contractor-classification adding separate surfaces.
sourceName: Toast WS-E India analysis
methodology: Statutory reading of the Act's 'online money game' stake-for-winnings definition (wse-india-2025-act-definition) against its 'person enabling ... or financial transactions' penalty (wse-india-2025-bank-ad-ban), plus wse-india-2025-skill-erased. Analysis only, not legal advice; no Indian case has tested paid-player labor. The crux is wage-vs-stake: the worker is paid to play and does not risk own funds for a return, but the platform funding the game and moving money is squarely within the enabling/financial-transaction prohibitions.
notes: Labeled analysis, tier D. Even under the most Toast-favorable reading (players = labor, untouched), the Act's collapse of the skill/chance distinction plus the bank/enabling prohibitions makes India a deteriorating, not stable, source of counterparty supply.

claim: Skillz CEO Andrew Paradise highlighted evidence submitted to the court that, he said, showed AviaGames using deceptive internal language to disguise its bots, referring to them by code names 'cucumbers' and 'guides.'
sourceName: GamesBeat
quote: Paradise highlighted AviaGames's use of deceptive language to disguise the presence of bots, referring to them as 'cucumbers' and 'guides' in evidence submitted to the court.
notes: 'Paradise' = Skillz CEO Andrew Paradise. Framed as a party characterization of trial evidence, not an adjudicated finding; the codenames were also reported in Bloomberg Law/Reuters discovery coverage but those pages were paywalled/403 to WebFetch.

claim: At trial Skillz alleged AviaGames misled players who believed they were competing against other humans but were in fact matched against company-controlled bots designed to beat human players wagering real money.
sourceName: PocketGamer.biz
quote: Skillz claimed it discovered that AviaGames was misleading players who believed they were competing against other humans but were being matched against bots that were controlled by the company and designed to beat the human players betting real money.
notes: This is the factual core of the 'is the opponent economically real?' question raised about Toast.

claim: AviaGames denied using bots to rig its games.
sourceName: Reuters (reprinted at CIPLawyer.com)
quote: AviaGames has denied using bots to rig its games.
notes: Company denial recorded for balance.

claim: AviaGames was subpoenaed by New Jersey federal prosecutors as part of a grand jury criminal probe into allegations it secretly used bot competitors to manipulate cash games advertised as human-only; the patent trial was delayed in light of the investigation.
sourceName: Reuters (reprinted at CIPLawyer.com)
quote: The judge said AviaGames had been subpoenaed by New Jersey federal prosecutors as part of a grand jury's probe into allegations that the company secretly used bot competitors to manipulate cash games that were advertised as having only human players.
notes: Reuters report dated Nov 28, 2023. This is the criminal-exposure analogue to Toast: covert non-genuine counterparties in a purported human-vs-human real-money game drew a federal grand jury.

claim: Skillz alleged AviaGames developed a 'shark robot' bot that targeted players who were winning too much in order to stop them from winning further.
sourceName: PocketGamer.biz
quote: developed a bot named a 'shark robot,' which targeted players who were winning too much to prevent them from winning further.
notes: Allegation by Skillz; goes to the theory that the house adjusts liquidity to protect its economics.

claim: U.S. District Judge Beth Labson Freeman, in California's Northern District, delayed the AviaGames patent trial until February 2, 2024 amid the pending criminal investigation.
sourceName: Casino.org
quote: U.S. District Judge Beth Labson Freeman in California's Northern District Court delayed the case against Avia until Feb. 2, 2024.
notes: Confirms court and presiding judge for the case.

claim: Because the jury found AviaGames' infringement willful, the court had discretion to increase the damages award up to three times the verdict.
sourceName: King & Spalding
quote: Because the jury found AviaGames' infringement was willful, the Court has discretion to increase the damages award up to three times the amount of the verdict.
notes: Willfulness exposure is the mechanism that likely drove the eventual $80M settlement.

claim: On April 15, 2011 ('Black Friday'), the DOJ unsealed an indictment against PokerStars, Full Tilt Poker and Absolute Poker naming 11 defendants, including executives and payment processors.
sourceName: PokerNews
quote: A federal indictment filed against PokerStars, Full Tilt Poker and Absolute Poker named 11 defendants, including PokerStars executive Isai Scheinberg, Full Tilt Poker's Raymond Bitar and Nelson Burtnick; Absolute Poker's Scott Tom and Brent Beckley and several payment processors.
notes: The 'nuclear precedent': an online real-money gaming model operating in a legal gray zone terminated overnight by federal criminal indictment (United States v. Scheinberg).

claim: The Black Friday indictment (United States v. Scheinberg) alleged that the defendants violated UIGEA and engaged in bank fraud and money laundering; a companion civil forfeiture case, United States v. PokerStars, et al. (11 Civ. 2564), sought recovery of roughly $3 billion in assets belonging to the poker companies.
sourceName: Wikipedia (United States v. Scheinberg)
quote: which alleges that the defendants violated the Unlawful Internet Gambling Enforcement Act (UIGEA) and engaged in bank fraud and money laundering to process transfers to and from their customers. ... A companion civil case, United States v. Pokerstars, et al., 11 Civ. 2564 (2011), included Full Tilt and Cereus (Absolute Poker), Ultimate Bet, Oldford Group, Rational Entertainment Enterprises, and many others as defendants and sought the recovery of forfeiture equalling approximately $3 billion in assets belonging to the companies.
notes: CORRECTED 2026-07-14: prior quote was a paraphrase, not verbatim (replaced with the article's actual sentence). Also corrected the claim: the ~$3B forfeiture figure was sought in the companion CIVIL case (United States v. PokerStars, et al., 11 Civ. 2564), not stated as a term of the criminal Scheinberg indictment itself — the original claim conflated the two proceedings. Both cases arose from the same April 15, 2011 'Black Friday' action.

claim: On July 31, 2012, the government dismissed the civil complaints against PokerStars and Full Tilt after settlement; the companies admitted no wrongdoing, and various individual defendants received prison sentences ranging from about 3 months to 3 years.
sourceName: Wikipedia (United States v. Scheinberg)
quote: On July 31, 2012, the US government dismissed "with prejudice" all civil complaints against all PokerStars and Full Tilt Poker companies after coming to a settlement with PokerStars which includes PokerStars purchasing Full Tilt. ... PokerStars and Full Tilt admitted no wrongdoing as part of the settlement
notes: Outcome: corporate survival via large settlements plus individual criminal exposure — a template for how these matters resolve. CORRECTED 2026-07-14: replaced a paraphrased quote with the article's verbatim sentence. Individual sentences per the same Wikipedia article: John Campos (Utah bank executive) was sentenced to 3 months; Brent Buckley to 14 months; Ira Rubin received a 3-year sentence — these anchor the claimed 'about 3 months to 3 years' range. Note for balance: lead defendant Isai Scheinberg himself was sentenced in September 2020 to a $30,000 fine with no jail time, so the range describes some, not all, individual defendants.

claim: California's AB 831 (Chapter 623, approved by the Governor October 11, 2025) added Penal Code §337o, which bans operating an online sweepstakes game and separately makes it unlawful for supporting entities — financial institutions, payment processors, geolocation providers, gaming content suppliers, platform providers, and media affiliates — to knowingly and willfully support, directly or indirectly, the operation, conduct, or promotion of an online sweepstakes game, extending liability beyond operators to the supplier/infrastructure layer.
sourceName: California Legislative Information — AB 831 (2025-2026 Reg. Sess.), chaptered text
quote: It is unlawful for any entity, financial institution, payment processor, geolocation provider, gaming content supplier, platform provider, or media affiliate to knowingly and willfully support directly or indirectly the operation, conduct, or promotion of an online sweepstakes game within this state.
notes: Added in Task 15 review fixes to back the synthesis/BRIEF statement that California's sweepstakes ban reaches vendors/suppliers, not just operators (research/wse-enforcement.md §4 asserted this from iGaming Business coverage without a ledger fact carrying the statutory language). Quote is verbatim Penal Code §337o(b) as added by AB 831; §337o(a) separately bans operating/conducting/offering an online sweepstakes game. Scienter limit: the bill's legislative-intent section confines reach to persons/entities that 'knowingly and intentionally engage in, promote, or facilitate' such games, excluding unknowing ancillary/public infrastructure — that limit matters when stretching the supplier-liability template to a B2B matching/liquidity provider like Toast (which licenses games via API to Sparket/GamerSaloon/Ember, see wsa-business-model-b2b-platform).

claim: Under the Bank Secrecy Act, a business that engages in the transfer of funds is a money services business (money transmitter) regardless of the dollar amount, and all MSBs must register with FinCEN and implement an anti-money-laundering compliance program.
sourceName: IRS Money Services Business Information Center
quote: A person who engages as a business in the transfer of funds is an MSB as a money transmitter, regardless of the amount of money transmission activity.
notes: Relevant if Toast is deemed to transmit value between US players and offshore counterparties; 'All MSBs are required to develop and implement an anti-money laundering (AML) compliance program.'

claim: Real-money and skill-gaming platforms that let users deposit, withdraw, or otherwise move funds/in-game value can be engaged in money transmission and become subject to Bank Secrecy Act AML/KYC obligations.
sourceName: InnReg (Fintech Guide to Gaming Compliance)
quote: If a platform handles user funds (deposits, withdrawals, in-game currency trades), AML rules probably apply.
notes: Practitioner guidance; also: 'Platforms that allow users to buy, send, hold, or cash out virtual currencies ... may be engaging in money transmission.' Cross-border payouts to India heighten SAR/OFAC/BSA and FEMA exposure.

claim: Effective October 1, 2023, India imposed a 28% GST on the full face value of deposits/bets in online real-money gaming, applied uniformly regardless of whether the game is one of skill or chance.
sourceName: Inc42
quote: a uniform 28% tax will be applied on the full value of bets placed in online games, regardless of whether it involves games of skill or chance
notes: First major Indian erosion of the skill/chance distinction — taxed identically. Reportedly raised affected companies' GST liability by roughly 1,000%, triggering layoffs and shutdowns.

claim: India's Promotion and Regulation of Online Gaming Act, 2025 (assented August 22, 2025) prohibits 'online money games,' defined as any digital game involving a payment or stake with the expectation of winning a monetary return.
sourceName: Legal 500 (thought leadership)
quote: any digital game that involves a payment or stake (in money or its equivalent) with the expectation of winning a monetary return.
notes: Enactment date Aug 22, 2025 per Wikipedia. The definition hinges on the player paying a stake for a monetary return — key to the labor-vs-wagering analysis of Toast's paid Indian players.

claim: India's 2025 Act separately criminalizes advertising online money games and bars banks/financial institutions or any person from enabling online money games or their related financial transactions.
sourceName: Legal 500 (thought leadership)
quote: any advertisement which in any medium of communication, including electronic communication, directly or indirectly invites or induces a person to play or participate in Online Money Games.
notes: The 'person enabling ... or financial transactions' language (imprisonment up to 3 years / INR 10M fine) is the hook by which a platform routing US-linked money to Indian players could be reached even if the individual players are treated as paid labor rather than wagerers.

claim: Under India's 2025 Act, offering an online money game is punishable by imprisonment of up to 3 years and/or a fine up to INR 10 million.
sourceName: Legal 500 (thought leadership)
quote: imprisonment of up to 3 (Three) years and/or a fine which may extend to INR 10 million (USD 1,14,611).
notes: Criminal, not merely civil, penalties on the operator/offering side. Quote corrected 2026-07-14 to include the '(USD 1,14,611)' parenthetical that sits between 'INR 10 million' and the sentence-final period on the source page; the prior quote silently elided it.

claim: India's 2025 Online Gaming Act (PROGA) treats all real-money games as prohibited, including established games of skill, erasing the long-standing legal distinction between games of skill and gambling.
sourceName: Yogonet International
quote: The Promotion and Regulation of Online Gaming Act, 2025 (PROGA) represents a fundamental policy shift because it treats all real-money games, including established games of skill, as prohibited.
notes: The single most consequential India finding for Toast: the exact 'it's skill, not gambling' argument on which Toast's US legality rests has been legislatively erased in the jurisdiction that supplies Toast's counterparty labor pool. Constitutional challenges are pending before the Supreme Court.

claim: On April 6, 2026, a divided (2-1) Third Circuit panel held that the CFTC has exclusive jurisdiction over Kalshi's sports-related event contracts, treating them as 'swaps' that field- and conflict-preempt state gambling law, and affirmed a preliminary injunction barring New Jersey from enforcing its gambling laws against Kalshi.
sourceName: Paul, Weiss (client memo)
quote: A divided panel of the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Third Circuit held that the U.S. Commodity Futures Trading Commission ("CFTC") has exclusive jurisdiction over sports-related events contracts offered by Kalshi
notes: The live analogue to the prediction-market analogy: a workaround model (prediction 'swaps' rather than 'bets') winning federal-preemption cover — the alternative to enforcement/legitimization is regulatory reclassification. Preliminary-injunction posture only; merits still to be decided.

claim: Federal courts are split on whether sports event contracts are CFTC 'swaps': district courts in Nevada, Maryland and the Southern District of Ohio held such contracts are not swaps within exclusive CFTC jurisdiction and/or that the CEA did not preempt state gambling laws.
sourceName: Paul, Weiss (client memo)
quote: the CEA did not preempt Maryland's gambling laws
notes: The workaround-model outcome is genuinely unsettled and circuit-dependent — a cautionary parallel for betting Toast's model will hold up everywhere.

claim: On August 4, 2016, Governor Andrew Cuomo signed a bill legalizing and regulating interactive/daily fantasy sports in New York, declaring them games of skill rather than games of chance.
sourceName: Gambling and the Law (Prof. I. Nelson Rose)
quote: interactive fantasy sports are not games of chance because they consist of fantasy or simulation sports games or contests in which the fantasy or simulation sports teams are selected based upon the skill and knowledge of the participants
notes: The legislative-legitimization endpoint of the DFS playbook: nine months after the AG shut them down, the legislature codified DFS as skill and regulated/taxed it (15% of gross gaming revenue; $50,000 registration fee). NB a NY appellate court later (2018-2020) held part of the law unconstitutional under the state constitution's gambling clause, showing legitimization can be fragile.

claim: In June 2025, New York AG Letitia James stopped 26 online sweepstakes casino platforms (offering slots, table games and sports betting via virtual coins redeemable for cash/prizes) by sending cease-and-desist letters; all 26 ended sweepstakes-coin sales in New York.
sourceName: New York State Attorney General
quote: sent cease and desist letters to the operators of these platforms, demanding that they stop all prohibited gambling activity in the state, and as a result, all 26 platforms are ending the sale of sweepstakes coins in New York.
notes: The live 'gray-to-enforcement' counterexample: a workaround real-money model (dual-currency 'sweepstakes' rather than skill) being shut down by AGs rather than legitimized.

claim: A proposed consumer class action, Pandolfi v. AviaGames (filed Nov. 17, 2023), accuses AviaGames of pitting players against computer bots in supposedly skill-based, human-vs-human competitions, and asserts federal racketeering (RICO) claims against its founders and investors.
sourceName: ClassAction.org
quote: pitting its online game players against computer bots in what are supposed to be skill-based competitions played against other human opponents.
notes: Case No. 5:23-cv-05971. RICO theory names co-founders Vickie Chen and Ping Wang and investors ACME and Galaxy Digital as backing an 'illegal gambling scheme' — the private-litigation mirror of the criminal probe.

claim: Papaya Gaming agreed to pay $15 million to settle a consumer class action (Barcelo v. Papaya Gaming) alleging it rigged Solitaire Cash, Bingo Cash and 21 Cash with undisclosed bots that mimicked human players.
sourceName: ClassAction.org
quote: Papaya Gaming Ltd. is set to pay $15 million to settle a class action lawsuit
notes: Case: Barcelo, et al v. Papaya Gaming Ltd. (No. 24STCV32626). Claim deadline Jan 30, 2026; final-approval hearing Mar 2, 2026. Separate from Skillz's competitor suit (wse-skillz-papaya-suit).

claim: Papaya told customers over 200 times that it does not use bots and closed the accounts of multiple players who expressed suspicion about bots.
sourceName: GamblingHarm.org (citing Reuters)
quote: Papaya told customers over 200 times that 'we do not use bots' and closed the accounts of multiple players who expressed suspicion about bots in their games.
notes: Affirmative misrepresentations of counterparty authenticity are the consumer-fraud hook a prosecutor/AG would use.

claim: In an October 27 ruling, a U.S. federal judge said it is 'undisputed' that Israel-based Papaya Gaming, maker of the Solitaire Cash app, used bots from 2019 until at least 2023.
sourceName: GamblingHarm.org (citing Reuters)
quote: In an Oct. 27 ruling, a U.S. federal judge said it is 'undisputed' that Israel-based Papaya Gaming, maker of the Solitaire Cash app, used bots from 2019 until at least 2023.
notes: A second major skill-gaming operator judicially found to have deployed bots in purported human-vs-human contests.

claim: On November 10, 2015, New York Attorney General Eric Schneiderman issued cease-and-desist letters to daily fantasy sports operators DraftKings and FanDuel, ordering them to immediately stop accepting wagers in New York as illegal gambling.
sourceName: New York State Attorney General
quote: Attorney General Eric T. Schneiderman issued cease-and-desist letters to daily fantasy sports ("DFS") wagering sites DraftKings and FanDuel on Tuesday, ordering both companies to immediately stop accepting wagers inside New York.
notes: The opening move of the DFS 'gray-to-regulated' playbook. Press release dated Nov 11, 2015; letters issued Tuesday Nov 10, 2015.

claim: Schneiderman characterized DFS as a scheme to evade New York gambling law, calling DraftKings and FanDuel the leaders of a massive, multi-billion-dollar scheme intended to evade the law.
sourceName: New York State Attorney General
quote: Daily fantasy sports is neither victimless nor harmless, and it is clear that DraftKings and FanDuel are the leaders of a massive, multi-billion-dollar scheme intended to evade the law
notes: The 'scheme to evade the law' framing is exactly the prosecutorial lens raised for Toast.

claim: Skillz Platform, Inc. v. AviaGames Inc. was brought in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California and asserted two Skillz patents, U.S. Patent Nos. 9,649,564 and 9,479,602, alleging AviaGames built its competing Pocket7Games platform using Skillz intellectual property obtained while developing games for Skillz.
sourceName: Sports Litigation Alert
quote: AviaGames maintains a competing mobile gaming platform, which AviaGames developed using Skillz's intellectual property that it gleaned while developing games for Skillz's platform. ... The two patents at issue are U.S Patent Nos. 9,649.564 (the 564 Patent) and U.S. Patent Nos. 9,479,602 (the 602 Patent).
notes: Docket No. 5:21-cv-02436 (N.D. Cal.) confirmed via Esports Legal News ('U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California, No. 5:21-cv-02436', https://esportslegal.news/2024/02/15/skillz-secures-usd-42-9-million-victory-in-patent-infringement-case-against-aviagames/, accessed 2026-07-14) — the sportslitigationalert.com page itself never names the court. Filing date corrected and confirmed as April 5, 2021 via Skillz's own SEC Form 10-Q (period 2024-03-31, filed 2024-08-29, https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1801661/000180166124000102/sklz-20240331.htm): 'a patent infringement action Skillz brought against ... AviaGames ... on April 5, 2021.' (Earlier note wrongly said the filing month was unavailable without RECAP; the SEC filing supplies it directly.) The patent number '9,649.564' (period) on the sportslitigationalert.com page is that source's own typo, not an OCR artifact; the correct number, 9,649,564, is confirmed by the same SEC 10-Q ('U.S. Patent No. 9,649,564, entitled "Peer-to-Peer Wagering Platform"'). Per the same sportslitigationalert.com article, the court granted AviaGames' 2022 motion to dismiss as to the '602 Patent without leave to amend while denying it as to the '564 Patent — consistent with the 2024 trial and verdict (fact wse-skillz-aviagames-verdict) concerning only the singular '564 patent, so the claim's 'asserted two patents' describes the original complaint, not what was tried. Judge Beth Labson Freeman per Casino.org (fact wse-aviagames-trial-delayed).

claim: Skillz and AviaGames settled the patent and unfair-competition litigation in April 2024, with Skillz receiving $50 million upfront in a deal totaling around $80 million (including licensing royalties).
sourceName: GamesBeat
quote: According to Paradise, Skillz received $50 million from AviaGames as part of a settlement agreement that totals around $80 million.
notes: Royalty component: $7.5M/year for four years starting the following year. Settlement dated on/about April 13, 2024 per Skillz SEC filings (SEC page 403-blocked to WebFetch). Settlement date, structure and royalty terms confirmed verbatim by Skillz's own SEC Form 10-Q: 'On April 13, 2024, Skillz, Big Run, and AviaGames entered into a settlement agreement ... AviaGames agreed to pay Skillz and Big Run a total of $80 million ... Skillz and Big Run have collectively received $50 million ... Beginning in March of 2025, AviaGames is required to pay Skillz $7.5 million annually for a four-year period.' (https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1801661/000180166124000102/sklz-20240331.htm)

claim: A unanimous U.S. federal jury (N.D. Cal.) on February 9, 2024 found AviaGames willfully infringed Skillz's patent and awarded Skillz $42.9 million in the Skillz Platform v. AviaGames patent case.
sourceName: King & Spalding
quote: On February 9, 2024, a unanimous jury found AviaGames' Pocket7Games app infringes multiple claims of Skillz's gaming patent. ... King & Spalding secured a $42.9m jury verdict in federal court for Skillz Platform Inc. ... Because the jury found AviaGames' infringement was willful, the Court has discretion to increase the damages award up to three times the amount of the verdict.
notes: Amount, date, unanimity and willfulness all verbatim on the cited K&S page (Skillz's trial counsel). Corroborated by PocketGamer.biz ('The jury awarded us $42.9 million and found willful infringement.') and the Skillz press release. Court identified as N.D. Cal. via Casino.org ('U.S. District Judge Beth Labson Freeman in California's Northern District Court', fact wse-aviagames-trial-delayed) and Esports Legal News ('U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California, No. 5:21-cv-02436'). THE most important precedent for Toast: a 'real players' skill operator found to have covertly matched humans against non-human opponents while marketing human-vs-human competition. Further corroborated verbatim by Skillz's own SEC Form 10-Q (period 2024-03-31, filed 2024-08-29): 'The jury found in favor of Skillz on all issues, determining AviaGames willfully infringed Skillz's U.S. Patent No. 9,649,564, entitled "Peer-to-Peer Wagering Platform" ... and awarding Plaintiff Skillz Platform Inc. ("Skillz") its full damages request of $42.9 million.' (https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1801661/000180166124000102/sklz-20240331.htm)

claim: On April 23, 2026, a jury awarded Skillz $420 million against Papaya Gaming in the S.D.N.Y. false-advertising case, reportedly the largest Lanham Act award in U.S. history, after finding Papaya misled users into believing they competed against real players when they were often matched against bots.

quote: A jury has awarded Las Vegas–based gaming company Skillz Inc. $420 million in damages against Papaya Gaming ... Papaya misled users by claiming they were competing against real players when, in fact, they were often matched against bots

Court found Papaya 'misled users by claiming they were competing against real players when, in fact, they were often matched against bots.' This is the decisive counterparty-authenticity precedent for Toast: misrepresenting who/what the opponent is now carries nine-figure exposure. Appeal status not reported.

claim: In 2006 Congress enacted the Unlawful Internet Gambling Enforcement Act (UIGEA), which prohibits gambling businesses from knowingly accepting payments connected to a bet or wager involving the Internet that is unlawful under any federal or state law.
sourceName: PokerNews
quote: In 2006, the US federal government enacted the Unlawful Internet Gambling Enforcement Act, or UIGEA, which prohibited gambling businesses from 'knowingly accepting payments in connection with the participation of another person in a bet or wager that involves the use of the Internet and that is unlawful under any federal or state law.'
notes: UIGEA (31 U.S.C. Sec. 5361-5367) attacks the payment rails, not the game — directly relevant to Toast's US-linked money flows to offshore counterparties.

claim: UIGEA came into full effect in 2010 and did not exclude games of skill such as poker, despite lobbying by the Poker Players Alliance.
sourceName: PokerNews
quote: The UIGEA went into full effect in 2010 and did not exclude games of skill like poker despite lobbying efforts from the Poker Players Alliance (PPA).
notes: Key caution for Toast's 'it's skill' defense: at the federal payments layer, a 'game of skill' label did not immunize poker; unlawfulness is determined by underlying state/federal law.

claim: Apple App Store Review Guideline 5.3 ('Gaming, Gambling, and Lotteries') as currently published flags this category as one of the most regulated on the App Store and requires developers to have fully vetted their legal obligations everywhere the app is available.
sourceName: Apple Developer — App Review Guidelines
quote: Gaming, gambling, and lotteries can be tricky to manage and tend to be one of the most regulated offerings on the App Store. Only include this functionality if you've fully vetted your legal obligations everywhere you make your app available and are prepared for extra time during the review process.

claim: Apple Guideline 5.3.4 requires that any app offering real money gaming (explicitly listed examples: sports betting, poker, casino games, horse racing) or lotteries hold the necessary licensing/permissions in every location where it is used, be geo-restricted to those locations, and be free to download; card-counting/gambling aids are banned outright. A skill-gaming app that mimics casino table games (e.g., blackjack) sits close to this boundary and Apple's classification of it as 'real money gaming' vs. 'skill contest' is a discretionary review call, not a bright line in the text.
sourceName: Apple Developer — App Review Guidelines, Section 5.3.4
quote: 5.3.4 Apps that offer real money gaming (e.g. sports betting, poker, casino games, horse racing) or lotteries must have necessary licensing and permissions in the locations where the app is used, must be geo-restricted to those locations, and must be free on the App Store. Illegal gambling aids, including card counters, are not permitted on the App Store. Lottery apps must have consideration, chance, and a prize.

claim: Physical/land-based casino blackjack tables deal roughly 50-60 hands/hour at a full table, up to about 100 hands/hour with fewer players; online casino blackjack (no physical shuffling/chip handling) runs faster, over 100 hands/hour generally and as high as ~200 hands/hour for single-player online play.
value: 50-60 (physical, full table); up to 100 (physical, few players); 100-200 (online)
sourceName: 666 Casino — "How Many Blackjack Hands Are Dealt Per Hour?"
quote: Full table: "around 50 to 60 hands per hour." Fewer players or head-to-head play: "up to 100 hands per hour." Online casinos, general play: "over 100 hands in an hour." Single-player online blackjack: "as high as 200 hands" per hour.
notes: Benchmark for a standard casino/live-dealer or single-player blackjack context; Toast's peer-to-peer matched-pair format (one US player, one paid India counterpart) is structurally closer to 1-on-1 or few-player play, so the upper half of this range (up to ~100-200/hr) is arguably the more relevant comparator for the per-hand model in wsf-toast-per-hand-model.

claim: Daily fantasy sports operators (DraftKings, FanDuel) typically retain a rake of roughly 10-15% of contest entry fees, with rake tending to be higher on small buy-in contests and lower on large guaranteed-prize-pool contests.
sourceName: WinDailyFantasy — "Daily Fantasy Rake is a Concern"
quote: "Usually, the percentage is roughly around 10 to 15%, depending upon the site and the size of the contest." DraftKings MLB $4,000-guaranteed-payout tournament example: "the entry is just $1, but they accept 4,600 people to enter the tourney... If everyone enters, the site will have profited $600 by holding that. That ends up being 15% of the entry fees that they keep in the form of their rake." FanDuel example: guaranteed payout of $4,500, $1 buy-in, up to 5,027 entrants — "If everyone joins, the rake equals $527, or about 12%."
notes: Corrected 2026-07-14: value/claim upper bound corrected from 16% to 15% — 16% does not appear anywhere on the source page; the source's stated general range is "10 to 15%." Also flagging (not correcting, since this is the source's own figure) an internal inconsistency in the source: the DraftKings example's entry pool is 4,600 entrants x $1 = $4,600 collected, so $600/$4,600 ≈ 13.0%, not the 15% the source states — the source appears to divide by the $4,000 guaranteed-payout figure rather than total entries collected. Quoted as the source states it; treat the DraftKings single-example percentage as approximate.

claim: Per percentage point of U.S. population reached, DraftKings' iGaming generated roughly 2.3x the Q4 2024 revenue density of its Sportsbook ($38.7M vs $16.8M per population-point); on a per-state basis the ratio is roughly 2.6x ($85.2M vs $33.0M per state) - a licensed casino-style product monetizes its addressable footprint at a meaningful multiple of sportsbook.
sourceName: Derived from DraftKings Q4 2024 disclosure via Sportico (own calculation)
methodology: Inputs (all from wsf-dkng-igaming-revenue-vs-sportsbook-q42024, tier B): Q4 2024 iGaming revenue $426M live in 5 states reaching 11% of U.S. population; Sportsbook revenue $825M live in 25 states reaching 49% of population. Per population-point: iGaming 426/11 = $38.7M per point; Sportsbook 825/49 = $16.8M per point; ratio 38.7/16.8 = 2.30x. Per state: iGaming 426/5 = $85.2M/state; Sportsbook 825/25 = $33.0M/state; ratio 85.2/33.0 = 2.58x (~2.6x). The population-point basis is the primary metric (state sizes vary widely); the per-state figure is shown as a secondary check. Caveats: single-quarter snapshot; iGaming states skew large/mature (NJ, PA, MI); revenue mix and promo intensity differ across products.
notes: Replaces an earlier '8x per-state revenue density' characterization in the research .md that did not recompute from the cited inputs and was removed in review.

claim: India's Parliament passed the Promotion and Regulation of Online Gaming Act, 2025 (Lok Sabha Aug 20, Rajya Sabha Aug 21, presidential assent Aug 22, 2025), which prohibits online money games and criminalizes offering/advertising them.
sourceName: Wikipedia — "Promotion and Regulation of Online Gaming Act, 2025"
quote: Passed by Lok Sabha: 20 August 2025 — Passed by Rajya Sabha: 21 August 2025 — Presidential assent: 22 August 2025. ... The Act "prohibits online money games and defines punishment for offering & advertising them."

claim: India's online real-money gaming ban took effect May 1, 2026, and its prohibition explicitly applies 'regardless of whether the game depends on skill or chance' if it involves entry fees/stakes/deposits/withdrawable monetary rewards — meaning it does not carve out games of skill, unlike the U.S. legal framework Toast relies on. Esports and social games with no monetary stakes remain legal under a new regulator, the Online Gaming Authority of India (OGAI). This is a first-order risk to any business model (including a hypothetical offshore India-based labor pool for a real-money app) that depends on India-based participation in real-money gaming activity.
sourceName: TFIPost — "India Bans Real-Money Online Gaming, Enforces New Digital Gaming Law From May 1"
quote: The law prohibits "all online money games. This includes any game that involves entry fees, stakes, deposits, or monetary rewards that users can withdraw or convert outside the platform." ... this restriction applies "regardless of whether the game depends on skill or chance" — meaning games of skill are equally banned if they involve real money. ... Esports and social games... "remain legal as long as they do not involve monetary stakes."
notes: Legal scope is genuinely contested: some secondary sources (e.g., a Wikipedia summary consulted in wsf-india-online-gaming-act-2025) characterize the Act as exempting 'games of skill' generally, while this source and several others in the broader search (law.asia, Lexology summaries, Yogonet coverage of pending Supreme Court challenges) describe a skill-agnostic ban on any money-staked game. Flagged for WS-D/WS-E follow-up; relevant to WS-F only insofar as it threatens the India labor-pool leg of Toast's cost stack modeled in wsf-toast-per-hand-model, not as a settled legal conclusion.

claim: Liftoff's 2025 Casual Gaming Apps Report found average iOS cost-per-install (CPI) for casino games of $21.03 versus $1.41 for casual games (roughly 15x higher), and $0.14 for casual games on Android — consistent with advertisers/UA buyers pricing casino-app users as having structurally higher LTV than casual-app users, which is indirect market evidence for (not proof of) the diligence LTV thesis.
sourceName: Liftoff.io — 2025 Casual Gaming Apps Report
quote: Costs per install (CPI) continue to be higher on iOS, with average CPI for casino games on iOS hitting $21.03. The average CPI for casual games was $1.41 on iOS and $0.14 on Android.
notes: CPI is a CAC proxy, not an LTV figure; higher CPI is consistent with (but does not itself prove) higher LTV — UA buyers could simply be over/mis-pricing casino installs. Presented as one data point among several for the LTV argument section, not conclusive.

claim: A widely-cited industry comparison (Andrew Chen) found online poker ARPU in Italy of roughly $73/month, versus $0.84-$9.30/month for a set of casual virtual-goods games (Second Life, Club Penguin, Habbo Hotel, Runescape, Facebook apps) — an 8x to 87x monetization gap between real-money skill/gambling play and casual free-to-play games, offered as directional evidence for the diligence 'casino-style play lifts LTV' thesis, though the underlying comparison predates mobile-native casino/skill games.
sourceName: Andrew Chen — "Gaming versus gambling ARPUs"
quote: Online poker players in Italy spend an average of $881 per person a year [~$73 ARPU/month]. Second Life: $9.30/month. Club Penguin: $1.62/month. Habbo Hotel: $1.30/month. Runescape: $0.84/month. Facebook apps: $1.20/month.
notes: Dated comparison (poker vs. 2000s-era virtual-goods games) rather than a direct casino-vs-casual mobile skill-game comparison; use as directional context, not a precise current benchmark.
