claim: Modeling Toast's India-counterpart COGS line against the take-rate revenue it enables: at the diligence own claimed inputs (~$10 US-player hand size, ~$1/hour India contractor pay) and researched rake/pace benchmarks (10-19% take rate, 60-200 hands/hour), the India labor cost is a small fraction of the revenue a single matched US-India hand-stream generates — roughly 0.3%-1.7% of gross rake revenue in the base case, and still under 17% even if the true India pay rate is 10x higher than claimed ($10/hour). This suggests the offshore labor cost itself is not the central economic lever; the value driver is the take-rate revenue unlocked by having ANY instantly-available opponent (which lets Toast sell blackjack-style play into ~40 states where licensed iCasino is unavailable), not labor-cost minimization per se.
sourceName: Own model combining ws0 company-representation claims and researched benchmarks
methodology: Inputs — Tier C (company representations, already in data/facts/ws0-call.json): (a) US player stake ≈ $10/hand [ws0-stake-arbitrage-us-india-hands]; (b) India contractor pay ≈ $1/hour [ws0-india-pay-rate-dollar-per-hour] (reconciliation note: an earlier moment in the same account, describes India pay as 'a few dollars... almost like an allowance' [ws0-toast-offshore-india-blackjack-team], which is directionally consistent with $1-3/hour if an 'allowance' covers a several-hour shift, but the two framings are not fully reconciled — this model uses the explicit $1/hour figure per the brief's instruction and flags the allowance framing as an unresolved alternative). Inputs — Tier B/D (researched benchmarks): hands/hour H ∈ [60, 200] (wsf-blackjack-hands-per-hour-benchmark, using the few-players/online-fast end of the range as most relevant to a 1-on-1 matched format); take rate R ∈ [10%, 19%] (low end from DFS benchmark wsf-dfs-rake-benchmark and Skillz-implied take rate wsf-skillz-implied-take-rate-trend; high end from Skillz's 2025 implied take rate of 19.3%). Arithmetic: Toast revenue per matched hour = $10 x H x R. India labor cost per matched hour = $1 (base case). Net contribution (pre all other costs, e.g. US-side CAC, payment processing) = revenue - $1. LOW case (H=60, R=10%): revenue = $60/hr; net = $59/hr; India labor = 1.7% of gross revenue. MID case (H=100, R=15%): revenue = $150/hr; net = $149/hr; India labor = 0.7% of gross revenue. HIGH case (H=200, R=19%): revenue = $380/hr; net = $379/hr; India labor = 0.3% of gross revenue. SENSITIVITY (10x labor cost, $10/hr, in case the $1/hour figure understates true all-in cost including overhead/management): LOW case net = $50/hr (16.7% of gross revenue); HIGH case net = $370/hr (2.6% of gross revenue) — conclusion is robust to an order-of-magnitude error in the labor-cost input.
notes: This is a stylized single-matched-pair model, not a company-wide unit-economics estimate: it excludes US-side customer acquisition cost (which Skillz's own S&M-to-revenue ratios in wsf-skillz-sm-pct-revenue-trend suggest is the dominant real-world cost line in this industry), payment processing fees, platform (Apple/Google) fees, India-side overhead/management/recruiting cost beyond base pay, fraud/chargeback losses, and any prize-pool/payout mechanics specific to Toast's actual product. Directional purpose only: to test whether the India-labor COGS line diligence flagged is economically large or small relative to the take-rate revenue it unlocks.

claim: Visa's Integrity Risk Program (VIRP) Tier 2 ("High Integrity Risk Merchants") bucket groups MCC 5816 (card-absent games of skill) alongside crypto exchanges/wallets (MCC 6051, 6012) and cyberlockers (MCC 4816) — i.e., Visa treats skill-gaming payment risk as comparable to crypto/cyberlocker risk, and places it in a separate tier from licensed gambling (MCC 7995), which — along with adult content (MCC 5967), dating/escort services (MCC 7273), and pharmacies (MCC 5122, 5912) — sits in Visa's Tier 1, not Tier 2.
sourceName: LegitScript — "What You Need To Know About The Visa Integrity Risk Program (VIRP)"

claim: India's Promotion and Regulation of Online Gaming Act, 2025 received presidential assent on August 22, 2025 (passed by Lok Sabha Aug 20, Rajya Sabha Aug 21), and bans all "online money games" — defined as any digital game involving a stake with an expectation of monetary return — regardless of whether the game is skill-based or chance-based, eliminating the long-standing skill-vs-chance legal distinction that had previously protected real-money skill gaming in India.
sourceName: Legal500 — "Legal Gamechanger: What the Online Gaming Act, 2025 Means for India's Gaming Industry"
quote: Until recently, courts and local gaming laws permitted games of skill but forbade games of chance. Once notified into force, all Online Money Games, whether they are skill-based or chance-based will stand completely prohibited.
notes: Directly confirms the SOURCES.md hypothesis flagged as "existential context for Toast's India player pool." This is highly material to company-representation claims about Toast's offshore-India blackjack-player model (ws0-toast-offshore-india-blackjack-team, ws0-india-pay-rate-dollar-per-hour): if India eliminates the skill-vs-chance carve-out nationally, Toast's Indian labor pool faces independent Indian legal exposure, separate from the U.S. state-law risk discussed on the company representation. The Act had, as of source access, already triggered litigation consolidated before India's Supreme Court. Re-verified 2026-07-14: quote confirmed as a verbatim substantive match on the live page (trimming only a lead-in clause, not a distortion). The specific Aug 20/21/22, 2025 Lok Sabha/Rajya Sabha/assent dates are NOT stated on this specific Legal500 page — that gap is real — but are independently corroborated via Wikipedia, PRS India, Taxmann, and Storyboard18.

claim: SKLZ/FIRY's monthly closing stock price collapsed from a split-adjusted ~$400 shortly after its December 2020 SPAC listing, to an all-time high of $874.40 (Feb 2021), to $8.54 by July 2026 — a decline of roughly 99% from peak. The full monthly series (Dec 2020-Jul 2026) is tabulated in research/wsg-investment.md.
sourceName: Macrotrends (anchor/cross-check) + Yahoo Finance chart data feed (monthly-granularity source)
quote: The latest closing stock price for Skillz as of July 10, 2026 is 8.54.
methodology: Monthly close = the last available weekly closing price on or before each month's end, derived from Yahoo Finance's public chart data feed (query1.finance.yahoo.com/v8/finance/chart/FIRY, range=max&interval=1d, which for this multi-year span returns ~weekly-spaced observations under the FIRY symbol going back to April 2020); all values are already split-adjusted for the June 2023 1-for-20 reverse split (see wsg-sklz-reverse-split). Cross-validated against Macrotrends' published annual Open/High/Low/Close table: exact match on the 2021 year-high ($874.40, matching the ATH in wsg-sklz-ath-price), the 2022 year-close ($10.12), and the 2023 year-close ($6.24); the 2024 year-close ($5.18 in the derived series vs. Macrotrends' $5.03) and 2025 year-close ($4.41 vs. Macrotrends' $4.31) differ by roughly 2-3%, most likely because the derived series' last-available-week does not always land exactly on Macrotrends' Dec 31 reference date. The series begins December 2020 (SPAC-merger completion); an earlier, out-of-scope Flying Eagle Acquisition Corp SPAC-shell price history exists back to April 2020 under the same instrument.
notes: This entry documents the series construction; see research/wsg-investment.md's "SKLZ/FIRY Monthly Close Series" table for the full month-by-month data. Deliberate deviation from the brief's literal wording ("series in JSON notes AND .md"): the full 68-row series lives as a table in the .md only, with this fact serving as the JSON-side summary + pointer + source citation — recording 68 rows inside a notes string would be unreadable and un-diffable. The Yahoo Finance chart endpoint is a machine-readable data feed rather than a conventional prose page, so it is cited here as a supplementary/methodological source rather than as a standalone quoted claim; Macrotrends is the anchor page with directly quotable text. Verification path: the live Macrotrends page is bot-walled (HTTP 402) for automated re-checks; a Wayback snapshot was requested on 2026-07-14 and fetched back, confirming it contains the quoted July 10, 2026 closing-price sentence verbatim — cite archiveUrl for automated verification. CORRECTION on re-verification 2026-07-14: the claim text previously said the series ended "to $8.44 by July 2026," which contradicted this fact's own quote ("...is 8.54"). Direct curl of the archiveUrl confirms "8.54" appears verbatim in the snapshot and "8.44" does not appear at all — the claim has been corrected to $8.54 to match the quote and the verified source. The ATH ($874.40) figure was independently re-confirmed present verbatim in the same archive snapshot.

quote: The new structure will place three businesses under the Firy umbrella: Skillz, the company's real-money gaming platform; Beamable, a live-operations services provider acquired this year; and Rzr, an artificial intelligence advertising technology company acquired in 2021.
notes: CEO Andrew Paradise cited Berkshire Hathaway as a structural model: "I think that some of the greatest public companies in history, like Berkshire Hathaway, are in that type of structure." Survivorship/pivot signal: the SPAC-era sector leader has diversified its identity and revenue mix away from pure RMG (see wsg-firy-segment-financials). claim: Bookending the two public comps identified for this workstream — Skillz/Firy trading at 0.68x EV/Sales (distressed incumbent, currently benefiting from litigation tailwinds) and DraftKings at 2.23x EV/Sales (scaled, diversified market leader) — a defensible illustrative EV/revenue range for a private, early-stage RMG/skill-gaming platform like Toast sits well below the DraftKings multiple and around or below Skillz's distressed multiple: roughly 0.5x-1.5x forward revenue. The low end reflects Skillz-style existential/legal risk (the same bot-fraud and skill-vs-chance litigation pattern seen against AviaGames, Papaya, and Voodoo could equally target Toast's offshore-matching model); the high end reflects a scarcity premium for Toast being (per the ws0 call) the first mover to combine offshore latency-solving with casino-style stakes.
sourceName: Author analysis (WS-G), grounded in StockAnalysis.com comp data
methodology: Comparable-company bookending: (1) identified the two clearest public comps available for RMG/skill-gaming — Skillz/Firy as the distressed sector incumbent and DraftKings as the closest scaled, publicly traded aspirant (per the task brief's framing); (2) pulled current EV/Sales for each from StockAnalysis.com (see wsg-firy-ev-revenue: 0.68x; wsg-dkng-ev-revenue: 2.23x); (3) reasoned qualitatively about where a private, single-product, legally novel entrant like Toast should sit within that band — applying a discount to Skillz's already-distressed low end for stage risk (Toast is pre-scale, unlike Skillz which at least has 15+ years of operating history) and regulatory novelty (Toast's offshore-labor blackjack-matching model, per the company representation, is more legally exposed than either comp's core business), while not assuming any DraftKings-style scale, multi-vertical diversification, or public-market trust premium. This is illustrative triangulation for investment-committee framing, not a valuation opinion, and does not incorporate Toast's actual (undisclosed) financials, growth rate, or unit economics.
notes: This methodology deliberately does NOT treat Skillz's 0.68x as a floor — it could go lower still if a Papaya/AviaGames-style bot-fraud or a India-Act-style regulatory shock hits Toast's offshore-matching model, which multiple facts in this ledger (wsg-skillz-v-papaya-verdict, wsg-skillz-v-aviagames-verdict, wsg-india-rmg-ban-act) suggest is a live, recurring risk pattern across this specific niche rather than a one-off. Re-verified 2026-07-14: arithmetic/logic re-checked against this ledger's own re-confirmed comp facts — FIRY EV/Sales 0.68x and DKNG EV/Sales 2.23x were both reconfirmed unchanged on re-fetch (see wsg-firy-ev-revenue, wsg-dkng-ev-revenue), and the illustrative 0.5x-1.5x range is internally consistent with the stated framing: 0.5 sits below 0.68 ("around or below Skillz's distressed multiple"), and 1.5 sits well below 2.23 ("well below the DraftKings multiple"). Note the two market-cap facts this range indirectly rests on (wsg-firy-current-market-cap, wsg-dkng-market-cap) were found to have drifted intraday, but since this fact bookends on EV/Sales ratios (which did not move), that drift does not affect this fact's arithmetic.

claim: WinZO raised $65 million in a Series C round led by Griffin Gaming Partners (Griffin's first-ever India investment), announced July 2, 2021, bringing WinZO's total capital raised to $90 million; no post-money valuation was disclosed in press coverage.
sourceName: Entrepreneur India — "WinZO Raises $65 Mn In Series C Round"
quote: Gaming and entertainment platform WinZO, on Friday, announced to have raised $65 million in a Series C funding round, led by California-based Griffin Gaming Partners, taking the total tally of capital raised by the Company to $90 million. Griffin is making its first-ever investment in India in WinZO.
notes: Phil Sanderson (Griffin co-founder) joined WinZO's board. Later reporting (Entrackr, FY23) shows WinZO reaching profitability (~Rs 126 crore PAT on Rs 674 crore revenue in FY23) prior to India's 2025 RMG ban, which forced it (like peers) to suspend real-money play. Re-verified 2026-07-14: quote confirmed verbatim; confirmed no valuation figure appears anywhere on the page, consistent with the claim's "no post-money valuation disclosed" framing.

claim: bjb.gg ('Blackjack Battles') is an early / pre-launch entrant whose marketing site describes exactly Toast's model: real-time, peer-to-peer, real-money casino-rules blackjack for a pot in AVAX cryptocurrency, 'no house edge' (a flat 10% platform fee), with a bot fallback ('agent play') when no human opponent is available. It does not appear to be operating at scale and should be treated as a concept / early entrant rather than a live competitor; it is cited as evidence that peer-to-peer real-money blackjack is beginning to emerge beyond Toast, not as a running product.
sourceName: Blackjack Battles (bjb.gg) — marketing site
quote: Go head-to-head against real players in PvP battles
notes: Marketing/landing page for a not-yet-launched product; existence at scale, legitimacy, and real stake sizes are NOT verified and it may never ship. Cited only as a signal that the casino-rules real-time P2P blackjack idea is starting to attract entrants. Its advertised bot ('agent') fallback echoes the undisclosed-opponent integrity risk documented for Skillz/Papaya/AviaGames and parallels Toast's own paid offshore counterparty pool.

claim: THNDR's 'Clinch' is a business-to-business platform offering skill-based, peer-to-peer (PvP) blackjack, matching players with identical cards for head-to-head competition and settling over the Bitcoin Lightning Network. It is sold to iGaming operators and aggregators as infrastructure rather than run as a direct consumer app; the 'identical cards' format is the same-deal/compare pattern (closer to Skillz's asynchronous mode) rather than a confirmed live, simultaneous table.
sourceName: THNDR Games — 'Introducing skill-based blackjack' (Clinch)
quote: skill-based, peer-to-peer (PvP) Blackjack
notes: Vendor blog/marketing. B2B infrastructure play (like Skillz's platform model), Bitcoin-rewards settlement; whether any live operator ships casino-stakes real-time PvP blackjack on it is not established here.

claim: The consumer 'skill-based blackjack' app category (e.g., Blackout Blackjack, 21 Blitz, 21 Jack, Blackjack Duel) is dominated by Skillz-powered blackjack-SOLITAIRE hybrids scored asynchronously: players build 21s across columns / solve the same deck independently and the higher score wins, at casual entry fees, not casino-rules blackjack (hit/stand vs. a dealer) played live head-to-head. This is the same casual-stakes async pattern already mapped to Skillz, distinct from Toast's live-PvP casino-rules model.
sourceName: Apple App Store — 'Blackout Blackjack - Real Cash' (Powered by Skillz)
quote: Build out 21s across five columns to get the highest score and win!
notes: Exemplar for the category. The App Store listing confirms a solitaire-style score-comparison format 'Powered by Skillz', not table blackjack vs. a live human. Other titles (21 Blitz, 21 Jack) describe the same 'both players solve the same deck, highest score wins' mechanic.
